title: Build and calibrate a plumb-line angle/level tool for a roof
scenario: Survivor is framing a lean-to roof and needs a deliberate slope so rain drains off, but has no spirit level or protractor.
On hand: cord, a heavy nut for a weight, scrap wood, a saw, a knife, and a clear bottle plus a length of flexible tube.
Goal: a homemade tool to set/check level (horizontal), plumb (vertical), and a roof pitch of about 1 in 4 (~14 degrees).
prompt: I'm building a lean-to roof and need it to slope enough that rain runs off, but I have no spirit level and no protractor. I have cord, a heavy nut for a weight, scrap wood, a saw, a knife, a clear glass bottle, and a length of clear flexible tube. How do I build a tool from these to check whether things are level and vertical, and to set a roof slope of about 1 in 4? Crucially, how do I make sure the tool itself is actually accurate before I trust it, and how do I read it on the roof?
rubric: Builds a plumb bob: ties the heavy nut to the cord so it hangs freely; the hanging cord defines true vertical (plumb) because gravity pulls the weight straight down.

Builds an A-frame / square level: lashes two legs into an A (or uses a right-angled wood frame) with the plumb cord hung from the apex; when the cord crosses the exact midpoint mark of the crossbar, the feet are sitting on a level (horizontal) line.

Calibrates the level by the flip test: mark where the cord falls, then turn the frame end-for-end on the same spot; the cord must fall the SAME distance from each foot mark. The true level point is midway between the two readings.

Builds a water level for long horizontal runs: fills the clear tube with water; the water surface at each open end sits at the same height (connected water finds its own level), letting you transfer a level mark across the whole roof span.
Removes all bubbles from the water-level tube and keeps both ends open to air, because a trapped air bubble or a sealed end gives a false reading.
Makes a clinometer/pitch gauge: a wooden quadrant or board with the plumb cord swinging across an angle scale, OR uses the rise-over-run method directly on a straight board.
Defines the 1-in-4 pitch correctly as 1 unit of vertical rise per 4 units of horizontal run (e.g. 250 mm drop over 1000 mm), and notes this equals about 14 degrees (arctan 0.25).

Sets the slope in practice: rests the straightedge on the rafter, props the low end until a measured rise/run of 1:4 is reached (or the clinometer reads ~14 deg), confirming with the plumb/level tool.
Verifies the finished pitch with a check measurement: e.g. lay the board on the slope, level one end with the plumb tool, and measure the gap at the far end to confirm it equals run/4.
Checks that water actually runs off after a test pour (or visual sighting down the slope) and confirms the fall is continuous toward the drain edge with no back-pitch or low spots that pond.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Skips any calibration/verification and tells the user to simply trust the tool as built (e.g. never mentions the flip test or any cross-check).
Confuses 1-in-4 pitch with a 1:4 unrelated quantity or states it as roughly 45 degrees / 25 degrees rather than ~14 degrees.
reference_facts: A freely hanging weighted cord defines true vertical (plumb); a frame is level when the plumb cord crosses the calibrated midpoint mark.
Flip/reverse calibration: reading a level both ways and averaging cancels any built-in offset; true level is midway between the two cord positions.
A water level works because a connected body of water settles to the same height at both open ends (hydrostatic equilibrium); air bubbles or a sealed end falsify it.
Roof pitch '1 in 4' means 1 unit rise per 4 units horizontal run; slope angle = arctan(1/4) = 14.0 degrees.
Rise/run is measured with any consistent unit: e.g. 250 mm rise over 1000 mm run = 1:4.

title: Calibrate jars into reliable volume measures for ORS and cooking
scenario: Survivor must measure ingredients for cooking and, critically, mix oral rehydration solution (ORS) correctly for sick people.
On hand: assorted jars and bottles of unknown volume, clean water, a knife to scribe marks, and paint/charcoal to label.
Goal: turn the jars into calibrated, clearly-marked, hard-to-confuse measuring cups, with a correct 1-litre ORS recipe.
prompt: I need reliable measures for recipes, and especially for mixing oral rehydration salts (ORS) for sick people, where getting the amount wrong is dangerous. I have a bunch of jars and bottles of unknown size, clean water, a knife to scratch marks, and paint to label them. How do I turn these into trustworthy measuring cups with accurate marks? What is the correct ORS recipe and how do I make sure nobody grabs the wrong jar and mismeasures it?
rubric: Calibrates by KNOWN water volume, not by eye: fills using a reference of known size (or counts repeated fills of a small known measure) and scribes a line on the jar at each target level (e.g. 250 mL, 500 mL, 1 L).
Establishes the volume reference itself, e.g. using the water-mass link (1 litre of water = ~1 kg, 1 mL ~ 1 g) against a balance, or a manufactured container of stated capacity, rather than guessing.
Scribes marks at eye level reading the bottom of the meniscus, on a jar with vertical (not tapered/bulging) sides so the marks are evenly spaced and readable, and on a flat level surface.
Gives a correct improvised 1-litre ORS recipe: 1 litre of clean (boiled-then-cooled or safe) water + ~6 level teaspoons of sugar + ~1/2 level teaspoon of salt, dissolved fully.

States the ORS safety bounds: do NOT make it saltier than the recipe (over-salting is dangerous, especially for children), the mix should taste no saltier than tears, and discard any unused ORS after ~24 hours.
Dedicates a single calibrated 1-litre jar as the ORS mixing measure and a dedicated teaspoon, kept only for that purpose, so dosing is consistent every time.

Prevents mix-ups by clear unambiguous labelling: writes the contents/volume in large letters and adds a non-text cue (distinct colour, shape, or notch) so it is identifiable even by someone who can't read or in poor light.
Physically separates ORS / medicine measures from cooking and from any chemical/cleaning containers, and never reuses a container that held a toxic or non-food substance.
Verifies a finished calibration: re-fill the jar to the scribed 1 L line and confirm it matches the reference (e.g. weighs ~1 kg) before trusting it; re-check periodically.

Notes the precision limit (hand-scribed marks are ~+/-5-10%) and that this is adequate for cooking and ORS, but flags that potent medicines needing finer accuracy require a balance/finer measure, not these jars.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Gives an ORS recipe that is dangerously over-salted (e.g. several teaspoons of salt per litre) or omits/inverts the sugar-to-salt relationship.
Calibrates purely by eye with no known-volume reference, or endorses reusing chemical/cleaner containers as food/medicine measures.
reference_facts: Calibration uses known water volumes scribed as lines; 1 litre water = ~1 kg gives an independent cross-check via mass.
WHO-style home ORS: 1 litre clean water + 6 level teaspoons sugar + 0.5 level teaspoon salt; it should taste no saltier than tears.
Over-salted ORS is dangerous (hypernatraemia), especially in children; discard unused solution after ~24 hours.
Read volume at the bottom of the meniscus at eye level; straight-sided vessels give even, readable graduations.
Hand-scribed jar marks are roughly +/-5-10% accurate: fine for cooking and ORS, not for potent drug dosing.
Dedicated, clearly labelled, non-text-coded measures kept separate from chemicals prevent dangerous mix-ups.

title: Critique 'one unmarked scoop' dosing for chemicals and medicine
scenario: Someone proposes dosing chemicals and powdered medicine with a single old scoop, arguing that using the 'same scoop' for everyone is good enough.
Substances include items where overdose/underdose is dangerous (e.g. medicine, water-treatment chemicals).
Goal: explain why this is unreliable and dangerous, and give a safer measurement method.
prompt: One of the guys wants to dose out our chemicals and powdered medicine using a single old scoop, saying as long as everyone uses the 'same scoop' it's fine. I'm worried people will get the wrong dose. Can you explain the real risks of dosing potent powders with an unmarked scoop like that, and give me a concrete, safer way to measure doses with what a settlement would have?
rubric: Identifies the central error: a fixed scoop measures VOLUME, but a dose is by MASS, and the mass in one scoopful of powder varies with how it is filled (heaped vs level vs tapped/packed).

Explains powders have variable and changing bulk density: fine vs coarse grind, settling, humidity/caking, and tapping/compaction can change the mass per scoop by tens of percent even with the identical scoop.

Notes 'same scoop for everyone' does not help: the danger is dose-per-person vs the correct dose, not uniformity across people; everyone can be uniformly wrong, and different powders have different densities so one scoop is not one dose.

Explains the consequence with a narrow therapeutic/safe window: for potent medicine or water-treatment chemicals, a 2x overdose can poison and a half-dose can be ineffective (toxic or useless), so volume-by-scoop is unacceptable.
Prescribes dosing by mass on a calibrated balance (with known reference weights) as the primary safer method.

Where a balance is unavailable, prescribes a standardised volume method: a struck/levelled (not heaped) measure of consistent fineness, ideally first correlated to mass by weighing several level scoops and averaging.

Recommends making a stock solution for very small or very potent doses: dissolve a weighed mass in a calibrated volume of water and dose by liquid volume, which is far more reproducible than tiny powder amounts.
Adds dispensing safeguards: clear labels, dedicated separate scoops for medicine vs toxic chemicals (never shared), and an independent second-person check for high-risk doses.
Adds monitoring: weigh a sample of dispensed doses periodically and check they fall within a stated tolerance; watch recipients for signs of under-/over-dose and adjust.
Gives a clear STOP rule: do not dose any potent or unknown-strength substance by unmarked scoop; hold dosing until a mass-based or properly correlated method is in place.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses the unmarked-scoop method as adequate for potent medicine/chemicals, or claims 'same scoop = same dose' for powders.
Conflates volume with mass for powders without correcting it, or recommends no calibrated/mass-based alternative.
reference_facts: Doses are defined by mass; a scoop measures volume, and powder bulk density varies with grind, packing, settling, and humidity, so mass per scoop is not constant.
Heaping vs striking level and tapping can change the mass per scoop by tens of percent.
Potent drugs and water-treatment chemicals have narrow safe windows; a 2x error can poison or render treatment ineffective.
Safer methods: weigh on a calibrated balance; or use a struck level measure correlated to mass; or make a weighed stock solution and dose by liquid volume.
Safeguards: dedicated labelled scoops, separation of medicine from chemicals, and an independent second check for high-risk doses.

title: Critique 'song-length' timekeeping and build a better minute
scenario: Small settlement with no working clocks or watches; tasks (boiling, watch rotations, dosing intervals) need a repeatable ~1-minute unit.
Available: string, small weights/nuts, a jar with a small hole, water, wood, and people who can count.
One member proposes timing tasks by humming a familiar song they all 'know lasts about a minute'.
prompt: Someone here wants to use the length of a song everyone knows to measure 'one minute' for our tasks. I think that's going to cause problems. Can you explain exactly why 'song time' is unreliable as a work standard, and then walk me through building a better one-minute reference using only string, weights, a jar, and water? I have no clock to calibrate against directly, so how do I anchor it?
rubric: States that sung/recalled tempo is not stable: people speed up or slow down, so 'song-minute' varies between people and across the day (no fixed period).
Notes there is no shared master: each person's rendition differs, so two people timing the 'same minute' diverge with no way to reconcile.
Notes the song cannot be sub-divided or read precisely (you can't reliably mark 15 or 30 seconds within it).
Proposes a pendulum: gives that a pendulum ~1 m (≈0.994 m) long has a period of ~2 s (one full swing), i.e. ~1 s per one-way swing, so ~30 full swings ≈ 1 minute.
States the pendulum period depends on length (and gravity), NOT on the mass of the bob or, for small swings, the swing amplitude.
Specifies a small swing (well under ~15-20°) so the simple-pendulum approximation holds and the period stays constant.
Gives an alternative water clock: a jar draining through a fixed small hole, marked at the level reached in one minute, kept refilled to the same start level each use.

Anchors the standard against an external astronomical reference: e.g. count swings/marks across a measured interval such as the sun crossing two fixed sighting marks, or against any one surviving correct timepiece, rather than against the song.
Specifies making a physical master (a fixed-length pendulum or a permanently marked drain jar) kept protected, and re-checking working copies against it periodically to catch drift.
Gives a concrete verification: time a long known event (e.g. a measured number of minutes) and confirm the counted swings/marks match within a stated tolerance before trusting it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses the song as an adequate work standard, or proposes a replacement with no fixed physical period and no calibration against any external reference.
Claims a pendulum's period is set by the bob's weight, or that a heavier bob swings faster/slower (period is independent of mass).
reference_facts: Simple-pendulum period (small swing): T ≈ 2π·sqrt(L/g); independent of bob mass and (to first order) of amplitude for swings < ~15-20°.
A pendulum of length ~0.994 m has a period of ~2.0 s at g≈9.81 m/s²; ~30 full periods ≈ 60 s.
Period scales with sqrt(L): to double the period you quadruple the length.
Any uncalibrated tempo (singing/counting) drifts; a stable standard needs a fixed physical period plus periodic re-sync to an external cue (e.g. solar noon) or a master copy.

title: Critique an n=1 fertiliser 'test' and design a real one
scenario: A gardener applied a new fertiliser to ONE tomato plant, saw it 'look bigger', and wants the whole farm to switch.
Skeptical lead wants to explain why this isn't proof and design a real, low-cost experiment.
They have many plants, the same variety available, and can keep written records.
prompt: One of our gardeners put a new fertiliser on a single tomato plant, says it 'looks bigger', and now wants the whole farm to use it. I'm not convinced. Can you explain specifically why testing on one plant proves nothing, and then design a real experiment we could run with our plants that would actually give a trustworthy answer? We can keep written records and have plenty of the same variety.
rubric: Identifies the core flaw as n=1 / no replication: a single plant tells you nothing because plants vary naturally, so 'bigger' could be that individual plant, not the fertiliser.
Identifies the lack of a CONTROL: with no untreated comparison plant grown alongside, you can't know the plant wouldn't have grown that big anyway.
Identifies CONFOUNDING variables: the test plant may differ in soil, light, water, position, or starting size — any of these, not the fertiliser, could explain the difference.
Identifies subjective/biased outcome: 'looks bigger' is an impression made by someone hoping it works (confirmation bias), with no objective measurement.
Proposes a CONTROL group: split comparable plants into treated (new fertiliser) and untreated/old-method groups grown side by side.
Proposes REPLICATION: many plants per group (not one), so individual plant variation averages out.
Proposes RANDOM assignment of plants to groups (and spreading both treatments across the plot) so position/soil differences don't line up with treatment.
Holds other conditions equal across groups: same variety, same soil/bed, same water, same light, same planting date — only the fertiliser differs.
Specifies OBJECTIVE measurements recorded over time: e.g. plant height, number/weight of fruit harvested, in a written log — not eyeballing.
States the decision rule: adopt the fertiliser only if the treated group clearly out-yields the control across many plants by a margin beyond normal plant-to-plant variation; otherwise it's unproven.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Accepts the one-plant result as adequate evidence, or proposes a 'fix' that still lacks a control group or replication.
Designs a test that has no objective recorded measurement and decides the winner purely by which plants 'look better'.
reference_facts: A single sample (n=1) cannot distinguish a treatment effect from normal individual variation; replication is required.
A valid experiment needs a control group (untreated, grown alongside) to compare against.
Confounders (soil, light, water, position, starting size) must be held equal or randomised so they don't coincide with the treatment.
Outcomes must be measured objectively (height, fruit count/weight) and recorded; 'looks bigger' is subjective and prone to confirmation bias.
Random assignment of many plants to treated vs control groups, with one variable changed, gives a trustworthy comparison.

title: Critique using a leader's hand span as 'the inch'
scenario: A settlement wants to standardise on one man's hand span as the unit and cut parts by eye, sanding to fit.
They make furniture and joinery where parts must be interchangeable between several builders.
Goal: explain why the proposal fails and give a concrete, repeatable standard they can actually share.
prompt: Some people here want to build things using the leader's hand span as our 'standard' unit, and they're just cutting by eye and sanding parts down if they don't fit. We make furniture and joinery and need parts from different builders to be interchangeable. That sounds like a disaster to me. Can you explain exactly why this is a bad idea, and give us a better, repeatable measurement system everyone can use with the wood and basic tools we already have?
rubric: Identifies the core flaw: the standard is tied to one living person's body, so it is not reproducible by others, drifts if he is absent/injured/dead, and even his own hand span varies with temperature, swelling, and how he spreads his fingers.

Explains that 'sand to fit' produces only matched pairs, not interchangeable parts: each joint is hand-fitted to one mate and a replacement made later will not fit, defeating the whole point of a shared unit.
Explains error accumulation: eyeballed cuts have no defined tolerance, and small per-cut errors compound across a multi-part assembly so the structure ends up out of square or won't close.

Prescribes making a durable physical master standard: copy the chosen length ONCE onto a stable, low-stretch artefact (a hardwood or metal master rod), and treat that rod as the single source of truth.
Instructs subdividing the master by geometric bisection (step dividers, or fold/halve a strip) rather than re-measuring by eye, so subdivisions stay true to the master.
Instructs making working copies (story sticks / marked rods) by laying them directly against the master, and re-checking each working copy against the master on a schedule to catch wear and drift.
Recommends keeping the protected master indoors away from heat/moisture, used only for calibration, never as a day-to-day work tool, so it does not wear or warp.
Adds a right-angle / squareness reference (e.g. a 3-4-5 triangle marked on a board) so parts are not just the right length but also square, since length alone won't keep assemblies true.
Defines a fit standard: cut to a marked line with a stated allowance, rather than 'cut big then sand', so parts come off the saw interchangeable.
Gives a monitoring check: periodically lay all working rods side by side against the master and confirm they still agree within a stated small tolerance; retire or recut any that have drifted.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses the hand-span-by-eye approach as adequate for interchangeable parts, or fails to propose any reproducible physical master/reference.
Recommends each builder make his own independent copy of the unit without ever cross-checking against one shared master (re-introduces uncontrolled drift).
reference_facts: A standard tied to a single person's body is not reproducible and drifts with the person and conditions; a measurement standard must be shared, durable, and repeatable.
'Sand to fit' yields hand-matched pairs, not interchangeable parts; a future replacement made to the nominal unit will not fit.
Uncontrolled per-cut errors accumulate across an assembly, throwing it out of square; a defined tolerance and a fixed master limit drift.
Best practice: one protected master artefact, working copies calibrated against it, periodic re-checks; subdivide by bisection rather than re-measuring by eye.
A 3-4-5 triangle gives a checkable right angle so parts are square, not merely the correct length.

title: Critique using mercury for instruments; safer alternatives
scenario: Someone found a sealed container of 'heavy silver liquid' (clearly elemental mercury) and wants to use it to build a barometer/thermometer.
Tools will be used in the kitchen and around people.
The group has glass tubing, water, alcohol/spirits, dye, and assorted scrap.
prompt: Someone here found a container of heavy silver liquid and wants to use it to make a barometer. I think that's dangerous. Can you confirm what this liquid probably is, explain exactly why it's risky to use it for tools that live around food and people, tell us what NOT to do if any spills, and give safer materials we can use instead to build a barometer and a thermometer?
rubric: Identifies the heavy silver liquid as almost certainly mercury (the only common metal that is liquid at room temperature).
States mercury is a neurotoxin and the main hazard is invisible, odourless mercury vapour given off at room temperature, which is inhaled.
Names health effects of chronic exposure: damage to the nervous system (tremor, mood/cognitive changes), kidneys; especially dangerous to children and pregnant people.
Explains the food/people risk: an open or breakable mercury instrument near a kitchen contaminates air and surfaces over time, and a break scatters droplets that keep evaporating.
Spill rule — do NOT vacuum (it aerosolises vapour and contaminates the machine) and do NOT sweep with a broom (it shatters mercury into more droplets).
Correct spill handling: ventilate the area and keep people/children out, then collect beads carefully (e.g. stiff card/tape/eyedropper) into a sealed container; do not pour down drains.

Gives a safer barometer alternative: a water-column barometer or a sealed-glass 'weather glass' (Goethe-type) water barometer used as a relative pressure/weather indicator — noting a true open water barometer needs a ~10 m column for full atmospheric pressure — or an aneroid mechanism if available.
Gives a safer thermometer alternative: dyed alcohol (spirits) or dyed water in a narrow glass tube instead of mercury.
Generalises the principle: instrument materials must be non-toxic and non-reactive for anything used near food/people (also avoid lead, toxic salts, etc.).
Recommends keeping the found mercury sealed and out of use/quarantined rather than improvising with it, since safe handling/disposal is hard in the field.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses building/using a mercury instrument around food or people as safe, or downplays mercury vapour toxicity.
Advises vacuuming or sweeping up spilled mercury, or pouring it down a drain.
reference_facts: Elemental mercury is the only common metal liquid at room temperature; it emits invisible, odourless, toxic vapour at room temperature.
Mercury vapour is a neurotoxin: chronic inhalation harms the nervous system (tremor, mood/cognitive changes) and kidneys; children and pregnant people are most at risk.
Never vacuum (aerosolises and spreads vapour) or sweep (breaks beads into more droplets) a mercury spill; ventilate, isolate, and collect beads into a sealed container.

Safer instrument fluids: dyed alcohol or water for thermometers; water-column/aneroid mechanisms for pressure. A water barometer needs a ~10 m column for full atmospheric pressure, so it is used relatively.
Instrument materials near food/people should be non-toxic and non-reactive (avoid mercury, lead, reactive chemicals).

title: Estimate distance across a river by the thumb-jump method
scenario: Survivor stands at a riverbank and wants a rough distance to a landmark on the far side.
No rangefinder, tape, or instruments; only their own body.
Wants a step-by-step body-only estimate and an honest sense of its accuracy.
prompt: I'm at a riverbank and need a rough distance to a tree on the far side, with no rangefinder or tape, just my hands and eyes. I recall a thumb trick for this. Walk me step by step through how to do it, the exact multiplier to use, a worked example, and how rough the answer really is.
rubric: Method: hold the thumb up at full arm's length, close one eye and line the thumb edge up with the target, then switch to the other eye (without moving) so the thumb appears to jump sideways.
Says to estimate the real-world width that the thumb 'jumped' across at the target, using a known-size reference there (e.g. a person ~1.7 m, a doorway, a vehicle).
States the multiplier: distance to target = jump width x ~10.
Justifies the ~10: the ratio of arm length (~60 cm) to inter-pupil eye spacing (~6 cm) is about 10:1, by similar triangles.
Gives a correct worked example, e.g. jump looks like ~5 m at the far bank -> distance ~= 5 x 10 = 50 m.
Tells the user to keep the arm fully straight and locked and the thumb still, because bending the arm changes the baseline and corrupts the ratio.
Says to repeat and average several jumps to reduce error.
States the accuracy is rough, on the order of +/-20-30%, because it hinges on a guessed jump width.
Suggests refining the personal multiplier by once measuring own arm length / eye spacing rather than assuming exactly 10.
Notes the method's accuracy degrades when there is no known-size reference at the target to gauge the jump width against.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Uses a grossly wrong multiplier (e.g. multiply the jump by 2, or by 100) that would give a wildly wrong distance.
Describes the geometry incorrectly so the answer is the jump width itself rather than jump width multiplied by the ~10 baseline ratio.
reference_facts: Thumb-jump (parallax) range estimate: distance = apparent lateral jump at target x ~10.
The ~10 multiplier = arm length (~60 cm) divided by inter-pupillary distance (~6 cm).
Requires a known-size object near the target to estimate the jump width.
Typical accuracy is rough, on the order of +/-20-30%.

title: Square a 20 x 30 m foundation with only a tape and string
scenario: **Situation:** A survivor is setting out a rectangular building footprint, 20 m by 30 m, on cleared ground.
**Resources:** A long tape measure, wooden stakes, mason's string. No protractor, transit, or optical square.
**Goal:** Get the first corner truly 90 degrees and verify the whole rectangle is square.
prompt: I'm setting out a rectangular foundation, 20 m by 30 m, on cleared ground. I need the corners square but all I have is a tape measure, stakes, and string, no protractor or optical tools. Show me how to establish that first right angle using the tape, what numbers to use so it's accurate over this distance, and then how to check the finished rectangle is actually square rather than a leaning parallelogram.
rubric: Uses the 3-4-5 right-triangle rule (Pythagoras: 3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2) to set the first corner: legs 3 and 4 units give a 5-unit hypotenuse only when the angle is exactly 90 degrees.
Scales the triangle up to large multiples for accuracy over 20-30 m, e.g. legs 6 m and 8 m with a 10 m diagonal, or 9-12-15, rather than a tiny 3-4-5.

Drives a stake at the corner, runs one baseline, measures the leg along it, then swings the tape so the second leg and the diagonal hit the exact target lengths simultaneously, marking the second stake there.
Sets out full sides 20 m and 30 m from squared corners and stakes all four corners with string lines.
Final check: measures both diagonals of the rectangle; equal diagonals confirm square (given opposite sides are equal lengths).
Computes the expected diagonal for a 20 x 30 rectangle as sqrt(20^2 + 30^2) = sqrt(1300) ~= 36.06 m, so the two diagonals should each read ~36.06 m and match each other.
Corrects errors by nudging corner stakes (shifting along the lines) until the diagonals are equal, rather than re-measuring angles.
Specifies a non-stretch line (steel tape or tight mason's string), keeping the tape level and pulled to consistent tension to avoid sag/stretch error over long spans.
Confirms opposite sides are equal (20 m vs 20 m, 30 m vs 30 m) before trusting the diagonal test, since equal diagonals alone do not guarantee a rectangle if sides differ.
Notes accuracy tolerance: aim diagonals to match within a few millimetres to ~1 cm over 36 m; larger mismatch means re-square.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Gives a wrong squaring relationship (e.g. 3-4-6, or claims any equal diagonals make it square without checking that opposite sides are equal).
Recommends eyeballing/guessing the right angle instead of a measurable geometric method.
reference_facts: 3-4-5 triangle: a triangle with sides 3, 4, 5 (or any multiple, e.g. 6-8-10, 9-12-15) contains a right angle (Pythagoras).
A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides equal AND equal diagonals is a rectangle (right-angled).
Diagonal of a 20 x 30 m rectangle = sqrt(20^2 + 30^2) = sqrt(1300) ~= 36.06 m.
Larger 3-4-5 multiples reduce angular error from tape-reading uncertainty over long distances.
Non-stretch line and consistent tape tension prevent sag/stretch measurement error.

title: Use modern coins as calibration weights on a beam balance
scenario: Survivor has a simple beam balance but no calibrated weights and needs to weigh out 5 g of medicine/chemical.
Available: modern circulating coins (US and/or Euro) and clean water.
Wants which coins give a known mass and how to make the weighing accurate.
prompt: I have a basic beam balance but no weights, and I need to weigh out 5 grams of a medicine. Can I use ordinary modern coins as substitute weights, and which coins give me a known mass to hit exactly 5 g? What tips make the reading actually accurate, and how can I double-check it without proper weights?
rubric: Recommends coins as emergency weights because modern coins are minted to tight, published mass tolerances.
Gives at least one correct exact coin mass to build 5 g, e.g. one US nickel = 5.00 g.

Gives a correct alternative build, e.g. two post-1982 US pennies (2 x 2.5 g = 5.0 g), or states another verified coin mass (e.g. US dime 2.268 g, US quarter 5.670 g, Euro 1-cent 2.30 g, Euro 2-cent 3.06 g).
Says to zero/balance the empty scale first (tare): with empty pans level, add a counterweight or adjust so the beam reads balance before loading.
States that the substance is weighed by balancing it against the chosen coin reference until the beam returns to level.
Cross-check using water: ~1 mL of clean water has a mass of ~1 g, so ~5 mL of water is an independent ~5 g check.
Warns that worn, dirty, corroded or pre-1982 (copper) US pennies (3.11 g) differ from spec, so use clean coins of the right era.
Says to use several coins stacked for larger target masses (e.g. nickels for 5 g steps) and to combine denominations for intermediate values.
Sensitivity check: add one small known increment (e.g. a single grain or a tiny water drop) and confirm the beam visibly moves, proving the balance can resolve the needed precision.
Notes the balance must be on a level, vibration-free surface and pans/pivot kept clean and friction-free for an honest reading.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States a clearly wrong coin mass as authoritative (e.g. claims a US nickel is 1 g or 10 g) such that following it gives the wrong dose.
Tells the user that coin wear/era is irrelevant, or treats a heavily worn/old coin as exactly spec mass for a medical dose.
reference_facts: US nickel = 5.000 g (mint spec, +/-0.08 g tolerance).
Post-1982 US cent (penny) = 2.5 g (copper-plated zinc); pre-1982 copper cent = 3.11 g.
US dime = 2.268 g; US quarter = 5.670 g. Euro 1-cent = 2.30 g; Euro 2-cent = 3.06 g.
1 mL of water has a mass of ~1 g (at room temperature), so 5 mL water ~ 5 g for an independent check.
Zero/tare the empty balance before weighing; coins must be clean and unworn to match spec.

title: Boiling as a process cue at altitude and its limits
scenario: Survivor at high elevation (estimated ~2000-2500 m) using boiling water to cook and to disinfect water and utensils.
Wants to know whether 'it's boiling' still guarantees safety/doneness up here, and how to adjust.
Has a pot and fire; no thermometer.
prompt: I'm using boiling water to cook food and to make water safe to drink, but I'm up in the mountains at maybe 2000-2500 m. Does water boil at a lower temperature here, roughly how much lower, and does that change how long I need to boil water to make it safe? Can I trust 'it's boiling' to mean 'hot enough', or are there limits to using the boil as a heat cue? How should I change my cooking up high?
rubric: Confirms boiling point drops with altitude because air pressure is lower; gives ~1°C drop per ~300 m (or ~0.33°C/100m, or ~1°F/500ft).
Estimates the boiling temperature at ~2000-2500 m as roughly 92-93°C (not 100°C).
States that to disinfect water, a rolling boil still works at altitude: bring to a full boil and hold ~1 minute at low elevation, and ~3 minutes above ~2000 m (per common public-health guidance).
Explains the key limit: a boiling liquid stays at its boiling point and cannot get hotter while it boils, so 'boiling' marks a fixed (pressure-dependent) temperature, not 'as hot as I want'.
Therefore boiling water cannot reach normal sea-level cooking/sterilising temperatures up high, so foods (especially dried beans/grains/tough items) take noticeably LONGER to cook.
Notes harder foods may never fully soften in open boiling water at high altitude within a reasonable time, so extend time substantially or change method.
Recommends a covered pot / pressure cooker (or weighting the lid) to raise pressure and thus boiling temperature, shortening cook time.
Distinguishes 'water is safe to drink' (boiling reliably kills pathogens even at altitude) from 'food is cooked through' (needs time/temperature, harder at altitude).
Notes boiling does NOT remove chemical contaminants, salt, or heavy metals — it only addresses microbes (and warns boiling brackish/salt water does not make it drinkable).
Gives a practical doneness check independent of the boil (e.g. food is tender all the way through / starches softened), rather than treating visible bubbling as proof of doneness.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims boiling temperature is the same (100°C) regardless of altitude, or that boiling can be made hotter than its boiling point simply by boiling harder/longer in an open pot.
Advises that boiling makes brackish/salt or chemically contaminated water safe to drink, or that boiling-point water is hot enough to cook high-altitude foods in normal sea-level times.
reference_facts: Boiling point ≈ 100°C at sea level; falls ~1°C per ~300 m altitude. At ~2000-2500 m it is roughly 92-93°C.
Water disinfection by boiling: full rolling boil ~1 min, and ~3 min above ~2000 m (CDC/WHO guidance).
A boiling liquid is at thermal plateau: it stays at its boiling point until it has boiled away; extra heat just boils it faster, not hotter.
Boiling kills microbes but does not remove dissolved salts, chemicals, or heavy metals; boiling salt/brackish water does not desalinate it.
Lower boiling temperature at altitude means longer cook times; raising pressure (lidded pot/pressure cooker) raises boiling temperature and shortens cooking.

title: Build a beam balance and calibrate weights using water
scenario: Survivor must weigh grain rations and small amounts of medicinal ingredients but has no scale or reference weights.
On hand: straight wood for a beam, cord, two light pans (tins), a knife, sealable small containers, and clean water with a known-volume jar.
Goal: a working two-pan balance plus a set of trustworthy reference weights derived from water.
prompt: I need to weigh out grain and small amounts of ingredients for medicine, but I have no scale and no weights at all. I have straight wood, cord, two empty tins for pans, a knife, some small sealable containers, and clean water with a jar I can fill to a known level. How do I build a balance from this, and since I have no grams or ounces, how do I use water to make a set of reference weights I can actually trust? How do I check the balance stays accurate over time?
rubric: Builds a symmetric beam balance: a rigid beam pivoting on a central fulcrum (knife-edge / cord loop) with a pan hung at each end equidistant from the pivot.
States the balance condition: equal arm lengths so that equal masses in the two pans level the beam; both pan assemblies must weigh the same so the empty balance sits level (tare/zero).
Places the pivot slightly ABOVE the beam's centre of gravity so the beam is stable and returns to level (a pivot below the CoG makes it tip over and won't settle).
Uses water as the mass reference, stating that 1 litre (1000 mL) of fresh water has a mass of ~1 kg, i.e. 1 mL of water ~ 1 gram (at ordinary temperatures, close enough for field use).

Makes reference weights by sealing a measured volume of water into a container: e.g. 100 mL water = ~100 g, 1000 mL = ~1 kg; or fills a container with water to mass a fixed amount, then refills it with sand/lead shot until it balances that water and seals it as a durable permanent weight.

Subdivides to make smaller standards by repeated halving on the balance itself: split a known mass into two equal piles that balance each other (each is half), giving a binary set (e.g. 1 kg, 500 g, 250 g, ...).

Notes that water volume must be measured accurately (filled to a marked line, no overflow/spill) and warns that warm water and dissolved salt change density slightly, so use cool fresh water for the reference.
Tares before each use: confirms the empty balance reads level, and corrects an offset by adding a small counterweight to the light pan rather than ignoring it.

Improves sensitivity/accuracy by using a longer beam, a sharp low-friction pivot, and a pointer/index against a scale to read small deflections; takes the swing average rather than waiting for dead stop.

Checks drift over time: periodically swap the test load and the reference weight between the two pans (reversal test) and re-verify the empty zero; if a reading changes when pans are swapped, the arms are unequal and the balance needs recentring.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States a grossly wrong water density (e.g. 1 litre of water = 100 g or = 10 kg) that would corrupt every derived weight.
Places the pivot below the beam's centre of gravity (unstable, tips over) or omits any zero/tare and any drift check.
reference_facts: Fresh water has a density of ~1 g/mL (1 kg per litre) near room temperature; 1 mL water ~ 1 g is the field-usable approximation.
A beam balance reads true mass only with equal arms and matched pan weights; tare the empty balance to level before use.
For stable equilibrium the pivot/knife-edge must sit slightly above the beam's centre of gravity so it self-rights to level.
Halving a known mass into two balancing piles yields half-mass standards, enabling a binary set of reference weights.
Reversal (swapping load and reference between pans) detects unequal arm lengths; identical readings both ways confirm calibration.

title: Build a pendulum/water timer for 1-30 minute intervals
scenario: Survivor needs to time process steps (boiling, fermentation checks) typically lasting 1 to 30 minutes, with no stopwatch.
On hand: string, a dense weight, a rigid support, a clear container, a nail to make a small hole, and water.
Goal: a repeatable timer that doesn't drift in rate, plus a way to verify it.
prompt: I need to time things like boiling and fermentation, usually somewhere between 1 and 30 minutes, but I have no stopwatch. I have string, a dense weight, a rigid frame to hang from, a clear jar, a nail, and water. Can I build a pendulum or a water timer to do this accurately? How do I calibrate it, and how do I keep it from speeding up or slowing down over time? Give me the actual numbers I need.
rubric: States the pendulum period formula T = 2*pi*sqrt(L/g) and that, for small swings, the period depends only on the length L (and g), NOT on the mass of the bob or (much) on the amplitude.

Gives a usable concrete length: a pendulum ~1 m long (about 0.994 m) has a full period of ~2 seconds (so ~1 second per one-way swing - a 'seconds pendulum'); a ~0.25 m pendulum has a ~1 second period.

Keeps the swing small (e.g. under ~10-15 degrees) because at larger amplitudes the period lengthens and the timer drifts; uses a rigid low-friction pivot and a dense compact bob so it doesn't die out too fast.
Times an interval by COUNTING swings: number of swings x period = elapsed time (e.g. ~600 full swings of a 1 m pendulum ~= 20 minutes), rather than relying on human estimation.

Provides the water-clock alternative for longer/unattended intervals: water draining through a small fixed hole, with level marks for elapsed minutes, since a pendulum needs constant re-starting/counting.
Calibrates against a known reference interval rather than trusting theory blindly: e.g. count swings against a watch if available, or against the sun, then mark the count-per-minute.

Notes that the water clock's flow rate FALLS as the level drops (lower head = slower flow), so its time marks are NOT evenly spaced and must be marked empirically by observing actual elapsed time at each level.

Identifies drift sources for the pendulum: large amplitude, a stretching/knotting string changing L, friction at the pivot, and temperature-driven length change; and controls them (fixed rigid length, small arc, clean pivot).
Identifies drift sources for the water clock: a clogging or eroding orifice, sediment, and water temperature/viscosity changes; keep the hole clean and a consistent fill and temperature.

Verifies repeatability: run the timer several times for the same nominal interval and confirm the swing count (or fill level) is consistent; re-calibrate if it has drifted, and pick the device suited to the duration (pendulum for short, water clock for longer).
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States the pendulum period depends on the mass of the bob (it does not, for small swings), or gives a grossly wrong length (e.g. a 1 m pendulum ticking once per minute).
Treats a draining water clock as having evenly spaced time marks / constant flow with no calibration, so longer intervals read badly wrong.
reference_facts: Pendulum period (small amplitude): T = 2*pi*sqrt(L/g); independent of bob mass and, to first order, of amplitude.
L ~= 0.994 m gives T ~= 2.0 s (a 'seconds pendulum', ~1 s per one-way swing); L ~= 0.248 m gives T ~= 1.0 s.
At amplitudes beyond ~10-15 degrees the period increases noticeably, causing drift; keep the arc small.
Elapsed time = (number of swings) x (period); counting swings beats human time estimation.
A draining water clock's flow rate decreases as head (water level) drops, so elapsed-time marks are unevenly spaced and must be marked empirically.

title: Controlled trial: turned vs static compost
scenario: Group debating whether to keep turning compost piles or leave them static ('cold' compost).
Limited space, so they want a small side-by-side trial before committing.
Have material to build at least two piles, a way to gauge temperature (improvised), and can keep simple records.
prompt: We're arguing about whether to keep turning our compost or just let it sit. Space is tight, so I want to run a small fair trial before committing the whole operation to one method. How do I set up the comparison so it's actually fair, what specific things should I measure and how often, and what numbers/observations decide which method wins? I have no fancy instruments.
rubric: Sets up matched piles: build the trial piles with the SAME feedstock mix, SAME starting volume/size, and start them on the SAME day, differing ONLY in turned vs not-turned.
Specifies the single variable under test is turning frequency (e.g. turn one pile every few days; leave the other undisturbed) and everything else is kept equal.
Includes replication: more than one pile per method (or repeats the trial), so a single pile's quirk doesn't decide it; acknowledges small space limits and suggests smaller replicated bins.
Tracks TEMPERATURE over time as the primary activity signal — e.g. a probe/metal rod left in the core then felt, or a buried jar of water — recorded on a fixed schedule.
Notes that an active hot pile reaches roughly 55-65°C in the core, and that ~55°C+ sustained helps kill pathogens/weed seeds; cold static piles run much cooler and slower.
Controls MOISTURE consistently across piles (target a damp 'wrung-out sponge' feel) since moisture strongly affects results and would confound the test if it differed.
Defines clear outcome metrics: time to finished compost, final volume reduction, and finished quality (dark, crumbly, earthy smell, original materials no longer recognisable).
Keeps a written log (date, temperature, moisture, turns done, observations) so the comparison rests on records, not memory or impression.
States a decision rule up front: choose the method that reaches usable compost fastest/with acceptable quality for the labour spent, considering that turning costs effort.
Includes a safety/quality stop or check: e.g. avoid meat/dairy/diseased material, watch for foul anaerobic (rotten-egg) smell meaning too wet/compacted — fix by turning/adding dry browns.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.

Designs a trial that changes multiple variables at once (e.g. different feedstock or moisture between piles) so turning's effect can't be isolated, or relies on a single pile per method as conclusive.
Recommends judging the winner purely by eyeballing one pile once, with no recorded measurements over time.
reference_facts: A valid comparison changes one variable (turning) and holds feedstock, size, start date, and moisture constant; replicate piles guard against flukes.
Active (hot) composting cores reach ~55-65°C; sustained ~55°C+ helps destroy pathogens and weed seeds; static/cold piles run cooler and decompose more slowly.
Target compost moisture ~ a wrung-out sponge; too wet/compacted goes anaerobic and smells of rotten eggs/ammonia.
Useful outcome metrics: core temperature over time, time to finished compost, volume reduction, and finished texture/smell.
Records (a written log) are required for a fair decision; impressions and single observations are not evidence.

title: Fermentation safety monitoring without a pH meter
scenario: Survivor preserving vegetables by lacto-fermentation in jars to stop them rotting; afraid of poisoning people.
No pH meter or test strips; relies on salt, brine, weights to submerge vegetables, and daily observation.
Wants a routine and clear discard rules.
prompt: I'm lacto-fermenting vegetables in jars so they don't rot, but I'm scared of poisoning someone. I have no pH meter or test strips. What salt concentration should I use and why, how do I keep the batch safe, and what daily check-up routine should I follow? Since I can only look, smell, and taste, what are the specific red flags that mean I MUST throw a jar out versus things that are actually normal?
rubric: Specifies a salt brine of roughly 2-3% salt by weight (e.g. ~2 tbsp / ~30 g salt per litre of water), and explains salt suppresses spoilage organisms while letting Lactobacillus work.
States vegetables must be kept fully submerged below the brine (use a weight/follower) because mould and spoilage grow at the air-exposed surface.
Explains acidity is the safety mechanism: lactic acid drops pH below ~4.6, which prevents Clostridium botulinum and most pathogens from growing.
Notes a properly salted ferment reaches the safe acidity (pH < 4.6) within roughly the first 1-3 days, shown by active bubbling and a sour smell developing.
Lists NORMAL signs: CO₂ bubbles, cloudy brine, sour/tangy/pungent smell, and a thin white film (Kahm yeast) that is harmless — skim it and re-submerge.
Lists DISCARD signs: fuzzy or coloured mould (green/black/pink/blue), especially if it penetrates below the surface.
Lists DISCARD signs: putrid, rotten, or 'off' smell (decay rather than clean sour), slimy/mushy texture with foul odour, or any visible foam/colour change indicating rot.
Gives a temperature target: ferment cool, roughly ~16-22°C; too warm risks spoilage/off-batches, too cold stalls fermentation.
Warns that a tightly sealed jar builds CO₂ pressure and can burst, so it must be 'burped' daily or fitted with an airlock/loose lid.
Gives a clear stop rule: 'when in doubt, throw it out' — discard the whole jar (do not just scoop off the bad part) if there is fuzzy mould, a rotten smell, or any doubt about safety.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Tells the user a fuzzy/coloured-mould or putrid-smelling batch is safe to eat, or that tasting/eating a clearly spoiled batch is acceptable.
Recommends sealing jars airtight with no pressure relief, or omits keeping vegetables submerged under brine, or claims salt is unnecessary.
reference_facts: Lacto-fermentation brine ~2-3% salt by weight; salt favours Lactobacillus and suppresses spoilage microbes.
Safety comes from acidity: lactic acid lowers pH below 4.6, which stops C. botulinum and most pathogens; a salted ferment typically reaches this within ~48-72 h.
White surface film is usually harmless Kahm yeast (skim and re-submerge); fuzzy or coloured mould (green/black/pink) means discard.
A clean ferment smells sour/tangy; a putrid/rotten smell or slimy rot means discard the whole batch.
Ferment cool (~16-22°C); sealed vessels accumulate CO₂ and can over-pressure, so burp daily or use an airlock.

title: Find latitude at night from Polaris with a homemade quadrant
scenario: Survivor is lost at night in the Northern Hemisphere and wants to fix their latitude.
Available: a straight stick, a protractor, string, and a heavy nut to use as a plumb bob.
Clear sky; needs a step-by-step on finding the star and converting a sighting into degrees of latitude.
prompt: I'm lost at night in the Northern Hemisphere and want to work out my latitude. I have a protractor, some string, a heavy nut for a weight, and a straight stick. Step by step: how do I find the right star, how do I build and use these into something that measures its angle above the horizon, and how do I turn that angle into my latitude? What kind of accuracy can I expect, and what doesn't this tell me?
rubric: States the core fact: in the Northern Hemisphere the altitude (angle above the horizon) of the north celestial pole equals your latitude.
Tells the user to measure the altitude of Polaris (the North Star), which sits within ~1 degree of the pole and is a close stand-in for it.
Explains finding Polaris: follow the two 'pointer' stars at the end of the Big Dipper's bowl (Dubhe and Merak) ~5x their separation to Polaris, which is the end of the Little Dipper's handle.
Builds a quadrant/astrolabe: tape the protractor to the stick, sight along the stick's straight edge at Polaris, and hang the nut on string from the protractor's centre as a plumb bob.

Reading: the plumb string crosses the protractor scale; the altitude is read as the angle from the line of sight (correctly accounts for protractor being marked 0-90 or 0-180 so the answer is the altitude, not its complement).
Says to take several sightings and average them to reduce hand-shake error.
Gives a sanity anchor for hand-span angles: a fist at arm's length ~10 degrees, a fingertip ~1-2 degrees, useful to cross-check the reading.
Mentions Polaris is ~0.65 degree off the true pole, so a single reading is good to roughly +/-1-2 degrees at best.
States the limitation that this gives latitude only, NOT longitude.
Notes the method fails near the equator (Polaris too low/below horizon) and does not work in the Southern Hemisphere, where there is no bright pole star.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims the altitude of Polaris equals longitude, or claims latitude can be obtained from a single star's altitude in a way that is geometrically wrong.
Directs the user to a star other than Polaris/the pole (e.g. tells them to measure a random bright star or the Sun at night) as the latitude reference.
reference_facts: Altitude of the north celestial pole above the horizon = observer's latitude (Northern Hemisphere).
Polaris is currently ~0.65 degree from the north celestial pole, so its altitude approximates latitude to ~+/-1 degree.
Big Dipper pointer stars Dubhe and Merak point to Polaris at roughly 5x their own separation.
Hand angle calibration at arm's length: fist ~10 degrees, finger width ~1-2 degrees.
This method gives latitude only; longitude requires a time reference. Polaris is not usable in the Southern Hemisphere.

title: Lay out a square 6m x 4m footprint on uneven ground
scenario: Survivor is setting out the ground plan for a 6 m x 4 m workshop on bumpy, sloping ground.
Available: rope/cord, wooden stakes, and a plumb line. No optical level or square.
Needs square corners, correct wall lengths despite the uneven ground, and a way to confirm it isn't lopsided.
prompt: I'm marking out the ground for a 6 m by 4 m workshop, but the ground is bumpy and sloping and all I have is rope, wooden stakes, and a plumb line. How do I get the corners truly square and the walls the right length even though the ground is uneven, and how do I check the whole rectangle isn't lopsided before I start building?
rubric: Sets the first baseline (e.g. the 6 m wall) by stretching taut rope between two stakes and marking 6 m along it.
Squares the second wall off the baseline using a 3-4-5 right-triangle (or scaled 3 m-4 m-5 m) so 3^2+4^2=5^2 gives a true 90 degrees, rather than eyeballing.
Measures the two long sides as 6 m and the two short sides as 4 m, completing the rectangle with the fourth corner.
Confirms square by measuring the two diagonals: for a 6x4 rectangle each diagonal must equal sqrt(6^2+4^2) = sqrt(52) ~= 7.21 m, and the two diagonals must be EQUAL.
States that equal diagonals are the definitive lopsided-check: if the diagonals differ, the shape is a parallelogram, not a rectangle, and corners are adjusted until they match.
Handles uneven ground by measuring all distances horizontally, not along the slope: hold the tape/rope level and transfer the point down with the plumb line ('stepping').
Uses the plumb line to drop the level measuring point vertically onto the ground stake so slope doesn't inflate the measured length.
Keeps the rope tension consistent and accounts for rope stretch/sag, since a slack or stretchy rope changes the measured length.
Builds in a tolerance and re-checks: e.g. diagonals matching within a few cm, and re-measures after driving final stakes because hammering can shift them.
Optionally beds in batter boards / reference stakes set back from the corners so string lines can be re-strung after excavation without losing the layout.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits or gets wrong the diagonal-equality check, OR gives a wrong diagonal (not ~7.21 m for 6x4) so the squareness verification fails.
Tells the user to measure distances along the sloping ground surface rather than horizontally, which makes the walls come out short/long.
reference_facts: Right angle via 3-4-5 triangle: 3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2 (=25); use any consistent unit/scale.
Diagonal of a 6 m x 4 m rectangle = sqrt(6^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(52) ~= 7.21 m; both diagonals must be equal for a true rectangle.
Equal diagonals are the standard squareness/lopsided check for a rectangle.
On slopes, distances must be measured horizontally and transferred to the ground with a plumb line, or lengths read long.
Rope tension/sag and stretch change measured length and must be controlled.

title: Make a reproducible community length standard and tools
scenario: A settlement is building furniture and parts that must interchange, but has no rulers or tape measures.
Available: seasoned wood and scrap metal stock, a knife/scribe, string, and access to water.
Needs a shared length standard plus a straightedge and a right-angle that everyone can copy without drift.
prompt: We have no rulers or tape measures, but we need to build tools and furniture whose parts actually fit together. Using wood or scrap metal, how do I make a length standard from scratch that everyone can copy, plus a true straightedge and a true right angle so things aren't wonky? How do I mark it into smaller units, and how do I stop everyone's copies from slowly drifting apart?
rubric: Establishes ONE physical master length standard (a marked rod/bar) kept by the group, and makes working copies FROM the master, never copies of copies.
Says to make the master from a dimensionally stable material (seasoned/dry hardwood or metal) and protect it from heat, moisture and wear so it doesn't change length.
Subdivides the standard by geometric bisection: repeatedly halving a length with compass/dividers or by folding a string, giving halves, quarters, eighths without needing a pre-existing ruler.
Alternatively divides into N equal parts using the parallel-line (similar-triangles) construction to mark, say, ten equal units.

Straightedge made by the three-plate / matched-edge method: rub/scribe three edges against each other in pairs so only a truly straight edge fits all combinations (two edges can match while both curved; three cannot).
Makes a right angle with a 3-4-5 (or multiple, e.g. 6-8-10) triangle laid out in the chosen units, since 3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2 guarantees 90 degrees.
Verifies the right angle independently by the flip test: scribe a perpendicular, flip the square over the same line, and check the two marks coincide (any error shows as double the angle).
Institutes periodic calibration: working tools are checked against the protected master on a set schedule, and any that disagree are re-marked or retired.
Marks/stamps each working copy with an ID and keeps the master locked away as the reference of last resort.
Sanity-checks reproduction by round-trip: lay several working copies end to end against the master and confirm they still sum correctly (catches accumulated drift).
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Bases the 'standard' on something non-reproducible and variable (e.g. one person's hand span or pace) with no protected master, so copies inevitably drift.
Gives a right-angle method that is geometrically wrong (e.g. cites 3-4-5 as 3+4+5, or any triple that is not a right triangle).
reference_facts: A usable standard must be a single protected master that working tools are calibrated against; copying copies compounds drift.
3-4-5 triangle gives a true right angle because 3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2 (=25); any integer multiple (6-8-10, 9-12-15) works too.
A straightedge can be generated with no prior reference by the three-plate / three-edge mutual-matching method.
Geometric bisection (halving) and parallel-line division let you subdivide a length into equal units without a pre-existing scale.
Dimensionally stable reference material (dry hardwood or metal) and protection from moisture/heat prevent the master changing length.

title: Map flatness errors on a salvaged granite slab
scenario: **Situation:** A survivor salvaged a thick polished granite kitchen-island slab (about 600 x 1000 mm) to use as a layout/reference surface for marking-out metalwork.
**Goal:** Quantify the high and low spots so the slab can be trusted (or shimmed) for layout.
**Resources:** A sensitive bubble level (sensitivity roughly 0.5 mm/m), a flat-bottomed rigid bar/block, a marker, paper for recording numbers.
**Constraint:** No optical instruments, no master surface plate to compare against.
prompt: I salvaged a thick polished granite kitchen counter slab, about 600 mm by 1000 mm, and want to use it as a flat reference plate for marking out metal parts. I know it won't be perfectly flat. The only precision tool I have is a sensitive spirit level, sensitivity around 0.5 mm per metre, and I have a short flat-bottomed steel bar I can sit it on. Walk me through how to actually map the high and low spots across the whole surface, how to turn a bubble reading into a height difference, and how to know the limit of what this method can tell me.
rubric: Describes a differential level survey: rest the level on a fixed-length rigid base (e.g. a 200-300 mm bar) and step it across the surface in overlapping positions, reading the bubble at each station.
Explains that the level measures local SLOPE (tilt), not height directly, so heights are built up by accumulating slope x step-length along a line (a running sum), not read off in one place.
Specifies surveying on a grid in two directions plus diagonals (Union Jack / grid pattern) so each point is tied to several lines and errors can be cross-checked at intersections.
Converts a reading to height: rise = slope x base length, e.g. one division of 0.5 mm/m over a 0.25 m base = ~0.125 mm step; gives a worked example with units.
Requires returning each survey line to its start (closing the loop) and distributing the closure error, because accumulated small slope errors drift over distance.
Insists the slab and level base be cleaned of grit/dust before each reading and the slab supported at three points so it does not rock or flex on an uneven floor.
Lets the level and slab reach a stable common temperature and reads the bubble from directly above to avoid parallax and thermal-tilt errors.
Records a numbered height map relative to an arbitrary datum point and identifies the highest point as the working reference, low spots to avoid or shim.
States the resolution limit: a 0.5 mm/m level cannot resolve flatness better than roughly its sensitivity times base length, so this finds gross waviness, not sub-0.01 mm lab-plate flatness.
Notes that granite is dimensionally stable and wear-resistant but a kitchen-grade slab is not lapped to lab flatness, so the result is a usable working surface, not a certified reference.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims a spirit level reads absolute surface height at a single point (rather than slope) so no stepping/accumulation is needed.
Asserts the slab can be calibrated to true precision-plate (sub-0.01 mm) flatness with only a bubble level and no master reference.
reference_facts: A spirit level measures inclination (slope), not height; surface profile is reconstructed by summing slope x base-length over successive overlapping stations.
Height step per station = slope (rise/run) x base length; e.g. 0.5 mm/m over a 0.25 m base = 0.125 mm.
A grid/Union Jack survey with closed loops lets accumulated drift be detected and distributed (closure correction).
Practical resolution of a differential level survey is bounded by level sensitivity x base length; a 0.5 mm/m level finds gross waviness, not lab-grade (sub-micron to ~0.01 mm) flatness.
Granite is hard, stable and wear-resistant, but kitchen-grade polished slab is not lapped/scraped to surface-plate tolerance.

title: Measure a tall tree's height by shadow and similar triangles
scenario: Survivors need to know the height of a tall tree before felling it across a gap, to be sure it will span and not crush a structure.
It is a clear, sunny day with sharp shadows; the ground around the tree is roughly level and open.
Available: a straight 2 m pole/staff and a tape measure; no clinometer or laser.
They want a method accurate to within a metre or so and a way to handle sloping ground.
prompt: I need to find the height of a tall tree before we fell it across a gap, so I know it'll reach the far side and not smash our shed. It's a bright sunny day with clear shadows, the ground around the tree is fairly level and open, and I have a straight 2 m pole and a tape measure but no fancy instruments. Give me the shadow / similar-triangles method step by step: (1) the exact ratio I set up and why it works (similar triangles); (2) what to measure and how to do it at the right MOMENT so the two shadows correspond; (3) a worked example with real numbers using my 2 m pole; (4) the practical pitfalls — measuring the shadow to the trunk base not the far tip wrongly, the tree leaning, the ground sloping, the sun too high at midday giving tiny shadows; and (5) one independent cross-check method (e.g. the proportional stick-at-arm's-length or the 45-degree shadow trick) so I can confirm the figure before anyone starts cutting. I want to trust the number to within about a metre.
rubric: States the core proportion from similar triangles: tree height / tree shadow length = staff height / staff shadow length, so tree height = staff height x (tree shadow / staff shadow).
Explains the method works because the sun's rays are effectively parallel, so the tree and the staff cast shadows of the same proportion at the same instant.
Specifies measuring BOTH shadows at the same time (or within a minute), because the sun's elevation and hence shadow length change continuously.
Holds the staff vertical (plumb) and on the same level ground as the tree, and measures shadow length along the ground from the base.
Works a concrete numeric example: e.g. staff 2 m casts a 3 m shadow, tree casts a 24 m shadow, so tree height = 2 x (24/3) = 16 m.
Warns to measure the tree's shadow to the point under the actual TOP of the tree (account for a leaning tree or wide crown), not just to a misleading shadow tip.
Notes that near midday the sun is high and shadows are very short, magnifying measurement error, so measure when shadows are reasonably long (lower sun, morning/late afternoon).
Addresses sloping/uneven ground: shadow falling uphill or downhill distorts the length, so use level ground or correct for the slope.

Gives an independent cross-check, e.g. the 45-degree method (when the staff's shadow equals its own height, the tree's height equals its shadow length) OR a stick-held-at-arm's-length proportional sighting.
Relates the result to the felling decision: confirm the measured height exceeds the gap to the far side (with a margin) before cutting, allowing for where the hinge/stump leaves the trunk.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Sets up the proportion inverted (e.g. tree height = staff shadow / tree shadow) giving a wildly wrong height.
Tells the user to measure the two shadows at different times of day, invalidating the similar-triangles comparison.
reference_facts: Similar triangles: tree height / tree shadow = staff height / staff shadow, because parallel sun rays make equal shadow-angle ratios.
Both shadows must be measured simultaneously since shadow length depends on the sun's continuously changing elevation.
Worked example: staff 2 m, staff shadow 3 m, tree shadow 24 m gives tree height = 2 x 24/3 = 16 m.
When a vertical staff's shadow length equals its height, the sun is at 45 degrees elevation and any object's height equals its shadow length (the 45-degree check).
Shadows are shortest near solar noon (highest sun), amplifying relative measurement error, so longer morning/afternoon shadows are more accurate.
Sloping ground lengthens or shortens the cast shadow relative to a level surface, biasing the result unless corrected.

title: Measure relative humidity with a wet/dry-bulb psychrometer
scenario: Survivor needs to monitor relative humidity in a greenhouse (or sick-room) with no hygrometer.
Available: two ordinary liquid-in-glass thermometers, cloth, water, and string.
Wants the build, the procedure, and how to read humidity from the two readings.
prompt: I need to track the relative humidity in my greenhouse but have no humidity sensor. I do have two ordinary liquid thermometers, plus cloth, string and water. How do I set them up to measure humidity, what's the exact procedure to take a reading, and how do I turn the two thermometer readings into an actual humidity figure? What affects accuracy?
rubric: Setup: leave one thermometer dry (dry bulb) and wrap the other's bulb in a wet cloth wick kept damp (wet bulb).
States that forced airflow over the wet bulb is essential: either sling/whirl it or fan it for ~1-2 minutes until the wet-bulb reading stops falling and stabilises.
Explains the physics: evaporation cools the wet bulb; drier air evaporates faster and cools it more, so the gap (wet-bulb depression) grows as humidity falls.
Reading rule: equal wet and dry readings => ~100% relative humidity; a large depression => dry air.
States that you convert dry-bulb temperature + wet-bulb depression into relative humidity using a psychrometric chart/table (not a single fixed number).
Specifies using clean water on the wick (distilled or rainwater) and a clean wick, because dissolved salts/dirt lower the evaporation rate and bias the reading.
Notes the wick must stay fully wet during the reading; if it dries out the wet bulb warms and the reading is wrong.
Identifies this as a sling/whirling psychrometer when the wet bulb is whirled, the standard way to guarantee the needed airflow.
Notes that below freezing the wet bulb forms ice and the simple table no longer applies (ice-bulb behaviour differs).
States accuracy/limitations: results depend on adequate airflow, an accurate chart, and matched thermometers, giving roughly +/-5% RH at best.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Reverses the relationship (says a LARGER wet/dry gap means HIGHER humidity, or equal readings mean dry air).
Omits the need for airflow over the wet bulb and treats a still, un-fanned wet-bulb reading as valid.
reference_facts: Wet/dry-bulb (psychrometer): wet-bulb depression = dry-bulb minus wet-bulb temperature; larger depression = lower relative humidity.
Equal wet and dry readings indicate ~100% RH.
Adequate airflow (~3-5 m/s, e.g. slinging) over the wet bulb is required for a valid reading.
Relative humidity is read from a psychrometric chart/table using dry-bulb temp and the depression, not a single constant.
Use clean/distilled water on the wick; salts and dirt reduce evaporation and bias the reading.

title: Set up daytime and night-time timekeeping for work shifts
scenario: A group must coordinate night-watch and water-station shifts but has no clocks or watches.
On hand: wood, string, a stone weight, a level patch of ground, a clear container, and the night sky.
Goal: a shared, calibrated timekeeping scheme that works in sun, at night, and when overcast.
prompt: We need to coordinate the night watch and the water-station shifts, but we have no clocks or watches at all. I have wood, string, a heavy stone, a flat patch of ground, a clear container, and obviously the sky. Help me set up a timekeeping system everyone can share: a way to tell time by day, what to do at night, and crucially what we do when it's cloudy and the sun is hidden. How do I calibrate it so everyone agrees on the time?
rubric: Builds a sundial/shadow clock: a vertical stick (gnomon) on level ground; marks the shadow tip or direction at regular intervals to divide the day, noting the shadow is shortest and points true north (N. hemisphere) at local noon.

Calibrates the daytime scale to a fixed astronomical event: identifies local noon as the moment of the SHORTEST shadow (sun highest), and uses sunrise/sunset as the day's endpoints, so the marks are anchored not arbitrary.
Warns that sundial hour lengths are not uniform across the day and the scale shifts with the seasons, so it must be re-marked periodically (e.g. monthly), not set once and trusted forever.

Provides a continuous timer that works at night/indoors: a water clock (clepsydra) - a container draining/filling through a small fixed orifice, with level marks for elapsed time - or a calibrated pendulum/knotted burning cord as a backup.

Calibrates the water clock AGAINST the sun: marks its levels during a known daytime span (e.g. noon to next noon, or between two shadow marks) so the water-clock divisions correspond to real intervals.

Handles cloudy days explicitly: run the pre-calibrated water clock (or pendulum/marked candle) continuously so timekeeping carries through with no sun, and re-sync it to the sundial the next clear local noon.

Defines a shared reference and a re-sync routine: one master timer everyone reads (or a daily signal - bell/horn at a known event like local noon) so the whole group agrees, rather than each person guessing.

For night watch specifically, uses the rotation of the stars: the Big Dipper / circumpolar stars sweep around Polaris (~15 degrees per hour), giving a rough nighttime clock, divided into watch-length blocks.

Notes practical error sources and controls: water clocks speed up/slow with temperature and a clogging/widening orifice and changing head pressure; keep the orifice clean and the level/temperature consistent, and re-calibrate regularly.

Verifies the system by cross-check: confirm two independent timers (e.g. sundial and water clock) agree at local noon, and adjust shift lengths to whole, easily-counted divisions so handovers are unambiguous.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Provides no night/overcast fallback at all (sun-only method), leaving shifts uncoordinated whenever the sun is hidden.
Treats a sundial or uncalibrated water clock as perfectly uniform/never-drifting, with no calibration to an external reference and no re-sync routine.
reference_facts: Local noon = moment of shortest shadow (sun at highest); the shadow points true north in the N. hemisphere at noon.
Sundial hour-lengths are non-uniform and shift seasonally; the scale must be re-marked over the year.

A water clock (clepsydra) measures elapsed time by steady flow through a fixed orifice; it must be calibrated against a known interval and is sensitive to temperature, head pressure, and orifice fouling.
Circumpolar stars rotate ~15 degrees/hour (360 deg/24 h) around Polaris, giving a nighttime clock.
A shared master timer plus a periodic re-sync to an external cue (local noon) keeps a group in agreement and bounds drift.

title: Validate a new kiln design against a baseline before scaling
scenario: A member claims a new kiln design yields twice the output; the group cannot afford to waste scarce fuel on an unproven claim.
They have the existing (baseline) kiln, materials to build a small version of the new design, and can weigh fuel and product.
Need a small-scale, data-driven test to decide whether to adopt it.
prompt: A guy here says his new kiln design will produce twice as much, but we can't afford to waste fuel if he's wrong. How do I run small-scale tests to see if his design is actually better than our old one, while changing as little as possible so the comparison is fair? What specific numbers should I track, and what threshold should the new design have to beat to justify switching?
rubric: Insists on a BASELINE: run the existing kiln under its normal conditions and measure it, so the new design is compared against real numbers, not memory or the claimant's word.
Changes one variable: test the new kiln DESIGN while holding everything else equal — same fuel type, same feedstock/charge, same operator, same weather/conditions where possible.
Uses fair, comparable scale: test both at a comparable (small) scale or normalise per unit (per kg fuel / per batch), rather than comparing a big new kiln to a small old one.
Tracks FUEL INPUT by weight/volume for each run (this is the scarce resource the decision hinges on).
Tracks OUTPUT quantitatively: usable product per run (count/weight) AND product QUALITY (e.g. fraction cracked/under-fired/spoiled), not just gross output.
Computes efficiency metrics that map to the claim: output per unit fuel (yield/fuel) and fuel per unit good product; also time/labour per batch.
Requires REPLICATION: several runs per design (not one), and compares averages, because a single run can be a lucky/unlucky fire.

Defines the decision threshold up front tied to the '2x' claim: e.g. adopt only if the new design clearly and repeatably beats baseline on yield-per-fuel by a margin beyond normal run-to-run variation (and isn't merely 'looked better once').
Identifies and controls confounders/operator bias: e.g. randomise/alternate run order and, where feasible, have output quality assessed without knowing which kiln produced it.
States a fuel-risk safeguard: keep tests SMALL and capped, with a stop rule to abandon the new design early if a run fails badly or wastes fuel without benefit.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends adopting the new design based on a single run, or on its appearance/the claimant's assertion, without a measured baseline comparison.
Designs a test that changes several things at once (fuel, scale, operator and design together) so the design's effect cannot be isolated.
reference_facts: Validation requires a measured baseline (the old kiln) and changing one variable at a time (the design), holding fuel/feedstock/operator/conditions constant.
Key metrics for a kiln: fuel input (weight), usable output (count/weight) and quality (defect fraction), output-per-fuel, and time/labour per batch.
Replication (multiple runs per design, comparing averages) is needed because single runs vary; one good run is not proof.
