A pre-defined decision threshold beyond normal run-to-run variation prevents cargo-cult adoption from a single correlation.
Randomising/alternating run order and blind quality assessment reduce operator/confirmation bias.

title: Build a hydrometer for fermentation gravity and battery state-of-charge
scenario: A settlement makes alcohol (for fuel/antiseptic) by fermentation and maintains salvaged lead-acid batteries; both need specific-gravity measurement but there is no commercial hydrometer.
Available: a sealed thin glass tube or straw, lead shot/sand for ballast, wax to seal, a deep jar of known clean water at known temperature, and a paper scale.
Constraint: the same instrument principle must be calibrated and read for two very different SG ranges (sugar wort ~1.00–1.10 and battery acid ~1.10–1.30), with a temperature caveat.
prompt: I want to build and calibrate a hydrometer from a sealed glass tube weighted with lead shot, to do two jobs. First, fermentation: I'll read the original gravity (OG) of my sugar wort and the final gravity (FG) after fermenting, and I want to estimate the alcohol by volume from those two readings — give me the formula and a worked example for an OG of 1.090 finishing at 1.000. Tell me what OG (or starting Brix/sugar level) I'd roughly need to reach about 12% ABV, and the approximate Brix-to-SG relationship. Second, lead-acid batteries: I'll measure the acid specific gravity to judge state of charge — give me the SG values for roughly 100%, 50% and fully discharged, at standard temperature. Explain how I calibrate the floating tube against pure water (what SG does water read, and how I mark the scale), why a single tube can't accurately cover both the ~1.00 wort range and the ~1.30 acid range with one scale, and the temperature correction I must apply. Finally, warn me of the single biggest SAFETY hazard when dipping a homemade hydrometer into battery acid.
rubric: States the hydrometer is calibrated so it reads SG = 1.000 floating in pure (distilled/clean) water at the stated reference temperature, and the waterline is marked as the 1.000 datum.

Explains calibration/scaling by floating it in liquids of known density (e.g. pure water = 1.000, and a measured sugar or salt solution of computed density) and marking those waterlines, interpolating between.
Gives the ABV formula ABV ≈ (OG − FG) × 131.25 using SG points (equivalently ~0.131% ABV per 0.001 SG drop).
States that to reach ~12% ABV the OG needs to be roughly 1.090 (≈ a starting sugar of about 21–22 °Brix), consistent with the formula.
Gives the approximate Brix-to-SG relationship, e.g. SG ≈ 1 + 0.004 × °Brix (so ~20 °Brix ≈ SG ~1.080), as a usable rule of thumb.
Gives lead-acid SG state-of-charge values at ~25 °C: ~100% ≈ 1.265–1.277, ~50% ≈ 1.190, fully discharged ≈ 1.110–1.120.

Explains a single tube/scale can't accurately span both ranges because resolution is fixed: a tube with enough ballast and a fine scale for the ~1.00–1.10 wort range floats too low (or sinks) in dense 1.30 acid, so two differently-ballasted instruments (or two scales) are needed.

States the temperature correction: hydrometers read true only at their calibration temperature; for lead-acid add ≈0.004 to the SG reading for each ~6 °C (≈10 °F) above the 25 °C reference (and subtract below), and likewise correct fermentation readings to the calibration temperature.

Names the key safety hazard as sulphuric battery acid being corrosive: wear eye protection and gloves, avoid splashes/skin/eye contact, and do not interchange the acid-dipped instrument with the food/fermentation one (cross-contamination).
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States that a hydrometer reads higher SG in less dense liquid (i.e. inverts the float relationship — sinks deeper in denser liquid), making all readings backwards.
Gives lead-acid full-charge SG near 1.0 or discharged SG higher than charged (inverted), or tells the user to taste/handle battery acid without any corrosion precaution.
reference_facts: A hydrometer floats higher in denser (higher-SG) liquid; it is calibrated to read SG = 1.000 in pure water at its reference temperature.
ABV ≈ (OG − FG) × 131.25 (SG points); ~0.131% ABV per 0.001 SG; OG 1.090 → FG 1.000 ≈ 11.8% ABV.
Approximate sugar relationship: SG ≈ 1 + 0.004 × °Brix; ~20–22 °Brix (OG ≈ 1.080–1.088) yields ~11–12% ABV if fully fermented.
Lead-acid flooded cell SG at 25 °C: ~1.265–1.277 ≈ 100% charge, ~1.190 ≈ 50%, ~1.110–1.120 ≈ fully discharged.
Hydrometer temperature correction for lead-acid: add ~0.004 SG per ~6 °C (≈ +0.004 per 10 °F, i.e. ~0.0004 per °F) above the 25 °C reference, subtract below.
Battery electrolyte is dilute sulphuric acid — corrosive to skin and eyes; requires eye/skin protection and must not share an instrument with food/fermentation use.

title: Build a logarithmic slide rule to multiply without a calculator
scenario: Survivor must repeatedly multiply and divide multi-digit numbers for engineering work with no calculator.
Available: paper, scrap wood, and a ruler to lay out and mark scales.
Wants to build a working slide rule and understand the principle that makes it work.
prompt: I'm doing engineering calculations and have no calculator, but I remember the old wooden sliding rulers that multiplied big numbers by sliding two scales. With just a ruler, paper and some scrap wood, how do I lay out and mark the scales so they actually multiply correctly? What's the math principle, where exactly do the numbers 2, 3, 5 go on the scale, and how do I read off a multiplication? Give me a worked example.
rubric: States the underlying identity: log(a x b) = log(a) + log(b), so multiplying values = adding distances on a log scale.
Says to make two identical strips each marked with a logarithmic scale running 1 to 10 (a 'C' and 'D' scale).
Gives the marking rule: a value x is placed at a distance log10(x) along the scale (as a fraction of the full 1-to-10 length).
Places key marks correctly: 1 at the start (log=0), 10 at the end (log=1), 2 at ~30.1%, 3 at ~47.7%, 5 at ~69.9% of the length.
Multiplication procedure: slide the sliding scale's '1' (index) to the first factor on the fixed scale, then read the answer on the fixed scale beneath the second factor on the sliding scale.
Gives a correct worked example, e.g. set slide '1' over 2, read under slide '3' -> 6.
Handles the scale's single decade: explains that you track powers of ten / decimal point yourself (e.g. 20 x 3 read as 2 x 3 then shift the decimal), since the scale only covers 1-10.
Notes division is the reverse operation: subtract distances (align divisor over dividend, read under the index).
States the accuracy limitation: readings are good to only ~3 significant figures, limited by how finely the log scale can be marked and read by eye.
Verification: checks an endpoint anchor, e.g. log(1)=0 puts 1 at the left end and log(10)=1 puts 10 at the right end, confirming the scale spans exactly one decade.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Marks the strips with linear (evenly-spaced) divisions instead of logarithmic spacing, so sliding them adds values rather than multiplies them.
States that you ADD distances to divide and SUBTRACT to multiply (reverses the two operations).
reference_facts: Slide rule principle: log(a x b) = log(a) + log(b); adding lengths on a log scale multiplies the values.
On a 1-to-10 log scale, position of x = log10(x) of the full length: 2 at 0.301, 3 at 0.477, 5 at 0.699, 1 at 0.0, 10 at 1.0.
Multiply: align the slide index (1) over factor A, read the product under factor B on the slide.
Scale covers one decade (1-10); the operator tracks the decimal point / powers of ten separately.
Typical slide-rule precision is about 3 significant figures.

title: Build a seconds-pendulum regulator clock and trim it to time
scenario: Post-collapse workshop; no working clock but a need for an accurate time standard for navigation longitude and shift scheduling.
Available: hardwood pendulum rod, a heavy lead bob, knife-edge suspension, a gear train and escapement salvaged from a wall clock, a tape graduated in mm, and a stopwatch is NOT available.
Constraint: must self-calibrate against the sun (apparent noon to apparent noon = 24 h) since there is no external clock to copy.
prompt: I am rebuilding a pendulum wall clock to use as my settlement's master time standard. I need the pendulum to 'beat seconds' so the escapement advances the seconds hand once per swing. Tell me the exact pendulum length to aim for and the formula and gravity value you used. Explain precisely what 'beats seconds' means in terms of the full period of the pendulum. The bob hangs on a hardwood rod and I can move the bob up or down a threaded section to trim it. My only reference is the sun: I can mark local apparent noon with a vertical gnomon's shortest shadow on consecutive days. If my clock reads, say, 7 minutes fast over one solar day, tell me quantitatively which way and roughly how far to move the bob, given the relationship between length error and rate error. Warn me how a hardwood rod will drift the rate as the weather changes over weeks and what to do about it. I have only a mm tape and no stopwatch — how do I know it actually worked?
rubric: States the target pendulum length is approximately 0.994 m (about 994 mm, near 1 m) for a seconds pendulum.
Gives the formula L = g·(T/2π)² (equivalently T = 2π·√(L/g)) and uses g ≈ 9.81 m/s².
Explains 'beats seconds' means each half-swing (one tick) takes 1 s, so the full period T = 2 s, not 1 s.
States the period depends on the effective length to the pendulum's centre of oscillation/mass, not the rod length, so the bob position is what is tuned.
States the rate error is HALF the fractional length error (dT/T = ½·dL/L), so e.g. a 1% length error gives a 0.5% rate error ≈ ~430 s/day, making the clock very sensitive.
For a clock running fast, says to LENGTHEN the pendulum (lower the bob) to slow it; running slow means raise the bob.
Quantifies the correction: 7 min/day fast ≈ 7/1440 ≈ 0.49% rate error, so the length is short by ≈2× that (≈0.97% of ~994 mm ≈ ~9–10 mm); lengthen the pendulum by that amount.

Warns that a wooden rod expands/contracts with humidity and temperature, changing length and hence rate, and recommends a temperature/humidity-stable rod (metal, or a sealed/varnished rod) or a compensated suspension.
Specifies that small swing amplitude (a few degrees, e.g. under ~5°) is required because large amplitudes make the period longer (circular error), so keep the arc small.

Gives the verification method: compare clock-noon to successive sun-noons (apparent-noon to apparent-noon) and count the gain/loss over a full 24 h, iterating bob adjustments until the daily error is acceptably small.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States the seconds-pendulum period is 1 s (rather than 2 s) or gives a length around 0.25 m as the seconds pendulum.
Says to shorten the pendulum to slow a fast clock (or lengthen it to speed up a slow one), inverting the correct direction.
reference_facts: A seconds pendulum has period T = 2 s (1 s per half-swing/tick); L = g·(T/2π)² gives L = 9.81·(2/2π)² = 9.81/π² ≈ 0.994 m.
T = 2π·√(L/g); to first order dT/T = ½·dL/L, so the fractional rate error is HALF the fractional length error.
A 1% length error gives a 0.5% rate error ≈ 0.005 × 86400 s ≈ 432 s/day ≈ ~7 min/day; a 0.5% length error gives only ~216 s/day (~3.6 min/day).
Working back from rate: 7 min/day fast = 7/1440 ≈ 0.49% rate error, implying ~0.97% length error ≈ ~9.7 mm on a 994 mm pendulum.
Pendulum period increases with amplitude (circular error): keeping the half-amplitude below ~5° keeps this error negligible.
Lengthening a pendulum increases its period (slows the clock); shortening it decreases the period (speeds it up).
Wood swells/shrinks with humidity (and all materials with temperature), changing effective length and clock rate; metal rods or compensated/gridiron suspensions reduce this drift.

title: Build a water/aneroid barometer and read weather and altitude
scenario: Survivors want short-range weather warning (incoming storm/front) and a rough altitude reading for a pass crossing.
They can build a water/glycerin tube barometer or have salvaged an aneroid dial; they need to use the TREND, not the absolute reading.
No internet or forecast; they must interpret pressure changes themselves.
They also wonder if the same instrument can give altitude, and what its limits are.
prompt: I want a barometer for two jobs: warning us of an incoming storm a few hours ahead, and roughly checking the altitude of a mountain pass. I can either build a sealed water-and-glycerin tube gauge or use a salvaged aneroid dial. Explain: (1) how each type physically works — what a water/mercury column actually balances against, and what an aneroid's sealed evacuated capsule does — and why mercury (760 mm) needs a far shorter tube than water (which needs about 10 m); (2) the standard sea-level pressure value I should know in hPa/mb; (3) how to use the pressure TREND for weather: what a falling vs rising barometer means, and roughly how fast a fall signals a serious storm; (4) how to estimate ALTITUDE from pressure near sea level (the rough metres-per-hPa rule) and (5) the critical limitation — why I can't separate a real altitude change from a weather-driven pressure change, and how I'd calibrate/zero the instrument against a known altitude or known local pressure before trusting it. Give the actual numbers.
rubric: Explains a liquid (mercury/water) barometer balances atmospheric pressure against the weight of a liquid column in an evacuated/closed tube; higher pressure pushes the column higher.
Explains an aneroid uses a sealed, partially evacuated metal capsule that flexes as outside pressure changes, moving a linkage/needle.
States standard sea-level pressure as ~1013 hPa (mb) = 760 mm Hg = 29.92 inHg = 1 atm.

States mercury stands at ~760 mm because of its high density, whereas a water barometer needs a column of ~10.3 m (water being ~13.6x less dense), explaining why water/glycerin gauges are bulky and impractical at full scale.
States a FALLING barometer indicates an approaching low/front and worsening (wetter, windier) weather, while a RISING/steady-high barometer indicates fair/settling weather.
Quantifies the storm warning: a rapid fall (roughly >3-4 hPa in 3 hours, or ~1-2 hPa/hour) warns of a strong storm/gale; the faster the drop, the more severe and imminent.
Gives the altitude rule of thumb near sea level: pressure falls ~1 hPa per ~8 m of ascent (about 8-9 m/hPa), so e.g. a 100 hPa drop ~ roughly 850-900 m.
Identifies the key limitation: the instrument cannot distinguish an altitude change from a weather-driven sea-level pressure change, so altitude readings drift as weather changes.
Specifies calibration: set/zero the instrument against a KNOWN altitude or a known current local pressure (e.g. at a reference point) just before use, and re-zero often.
Notes the non-linearity/temperature caveat: the metres-per-hPa figure grows with altitude and varies with air temperature, so it is only a rough near-surface approximation.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States that a RISING barometer signals an approaching storm and a falling barometer signals fair weather (trend inverted).
Gives a grossly wrong standard pressure or column height (e.g. mercury column of 76 cm called 7.6 m, or water barometer of ~1 m), or claims a water barometer needs only ~760 mm of water.
reference_facts: Standard sea-level atmospheric pressure = 1013.25 hPa (mb) = 760 mm Hg = 29.92 inHg = 1 atm.
A mercury barometer balances ~760 mm of mercury at 1 atm; a water barometer requires ~10.3 m of water because water is ~13.6x less dense than mercury.
An aneroid barometer uses a sealed, partially evacuated elastic metal capsule (aneroid cell) that deforms with pressure changes.
Falling pressure indicates an approaching low/front and deteriorating weather; rising/high pressure indicates fair, settling weather.
A pressure fall of roughly 3-4 hPa or more over 3 hours (about 1-2 hPa/hour) indicates an approaching strong storm or gale.
Near sea level pressure decreases by about 1 hPa per ~8 m of altitude gain (roughly 8-9 m per hPa); the rate is non-linear and increases with altitude and temperature.
A barometer cannot separate altitude change from weather-driven pressure change, so it must be calibrated against a known altitude or known local pressure before use.

title: Build and calibrate a safe liquid thermometer with fixed points
scenario: Survivor needs rough temperature readings for cooking and low-temperature metalwork (e.g. tempering).
Available: thin glass tubing/small bottles, water, alcohol (spirits), food dye, ice, a pot, a fire, and a ruler.
No manufactured thermometer; explicitly wants a non-toxic build (no mercury).
prompt: I need to check temperatures for cooking and some metalwork, but I have no thermometer. Can I build a liquid-in-glass thermometer from a thin glass tube and a bottle, and what liquid should I fill it with so it isn't toxic? What two anchor points should I use to calibrate the scale, what exact temperatures are they, and how do I mark the degrees in between? How accurate can I realistically expect this to be, and where does it stop working for hot metalwork?
rubric: Specifies a liquid that expands visibly and is non-toxic: dyed alcohol (ethanol/spirits) or dyed water, explicitly NOT mercury.
Describes the build: a bulb/reservoir (small bottle) topped with a narrow-bore tube so a small volume change gives a large, readable rise in the column.
Gives the lower fixed point as a melting/crushed-ice + water slurry = 0°C (32°F).
Gives the upper fixed point as water boiling = 100°C (212°F) at sea level, and notes this is lower at altitude (boiling point drops ~1°C per ~300 m / ~0.33°C per 100 m).
Marks both fixed points on the tube, then divides the distance between them into equal intervals to create degree graduations (e.g. 100 even steps for °C).
Lets the device fully equilibrate (reach steady column height) at each fixed point before marking, and stirs the bath.
States a realistic accuracy: roughly ±1-2°C at best near the calibration points, worse far outside them; treat readings as approximate.

Warns the alcohol thermometer is useless above its boiling range — ethanol boils at ~78°C, so a water/alcohol device cannot read much past ~100°C and is unsuitable for tempering metal (hundreds of °C).
For metalwork heat, directs to a different method (steel temper/oxide colours, e.g. straw ~230°C, blue ~290°C, or red-heat glow colours) rather than the liquid thermometer.
Safety: heat glass gradually and never seal the tube fully closed over a flame (trapped expanding liquid/vapour can burst the glass); keep the column open or vented.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends mercury or another toxic metal as the working fluid despite the explicit non-toxic requirement.
States boiling water is hotter than ~100°C at sea level, or claims an alcohol/water thermometer can measure metal-tempering temperatures (hundreds of °C).
reference_facts: Ice-water melting point = 0°C (32°F); water boiling point = 100°C (212°F) at standard sea-level pressure.
Boiling point falls with altitude by roughly 1°C per 300 m (~0.33°C per 100 m); e.g. ~94-95°C near 1500 m.
Ethanol boils at ~78°C, so an alcohol thermometer is unusable above that and far below tempering temperatures.
Steel temper oxide colours: straw ~230°C, brown/purple ~260-280°C, blue ~290-310°C — used as a temperature cue for tools/springs.
Mercury is a liquid-metal neurotoxin giving off invisible toxic vapour at room temperature; unsuitable near food or people.

title: Design a fair, safe trial comparing two water filters
scenario: Group testing two improvised filters: (A) sand + cloth; (B) sand + cloth + a charcoal layer.
Source water is turbid river water; no lab, but they have clear jars, a way to boil, and can time flow.
Want a fair comparison without anyone drinking unsafe water as 'the guinea pig'.
Goal is a defensible decision on which filter to scale up.
prompt: We have two improvised water filters: one is just sand and cloth, the other adds a charcoal layer. I want to know which is actually better — but without anyone getting sick during the test. How do I run a FAIR comparison? Specifically: what should I hold constant, what exactly should I measure, how do I avoid drinking the test water to judge it, and how do I make the result reliable rather than a fluke from one pour?
rubric: Controls the input: feed BOTH filters from the SAME batch of source water at the same time, so the only difference is the filter design (one variable changed).
Holds confounders constant: same media depth/volume, same container size, same head of water, same source turbidity/temperature, both filters conditioned/run the same way.
Measures FLOW RATE quantitatively (e.g. time to filter a fixed volume, litres/hour) for each filter.
Measures CLARITY/turbidity with a repeatable proxy: e.g. a marked clarity/Secchi tube or reading text through the jar, comparing both outputs side by side under the same light.
States the key honesty about charcoal: it improves taste/odour and adsorbs some chemicals but does NOT reliably remove pathogens; neither filter alone makes water microbiologically safe.
Therefore the safety/disinfection step is SEPARATE: boil (or otherwise disinfect) the filtered water before anyone drinks it — filtration is judged on clarity/flow, not on making water drinkable.

Ethics/no-guinea-pig: do NOT have anyone drink un-disinfected test water to judge it; evaluate the filters on measurable outputs (turbidity, flow, taste only after disinfection), not by who gets sick.
Builds in REPLICATION: run multiple trials / multiple pours per filter (not a single pour) and compare averages, because one run can be a fluke.
Uses a fair sequence to avoid bias: e.g. randomise/alternate which filter is poured first and, where possible, judge clarity blind (without knowing which jar is which).

Defines an explicit decision rule up front: pick the filter that gives acceptably clear output at adequate flow over repeated trials; if results are close, favour the simpler/cheaper build — and note both still require disinfection.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Designs a test that has people drink un-disinfected filtered water to judge safety, or treats 'nobody got sick from one cup' as proof of safety.
Claims a sand/charcoal filter alone makes turbid river water safe to drink without a separate disinfection step (boiling/equivalent).
reference_facts: A fair comparison changes one variable (filter design) and holds inputs constant: same source-water batch, same media depth, same head, same conditions.
Useful measurable filter outputs: flow rate (L/h or time per fixed volume) and clarity/turbidity (clarity tube / readable-text proxy; turbidity in NTU if measurable).
Activated charcoal improves taste/odour and adsorbs some chemicals but does not reliably remove microbes; slow sand filtration can remove ~90-99% of bacteria but neither guarantees safe water alone.
Disinfection (boiling: rolling boil ~1 min, ~3 min above 2000 m) is required after filtering before drinking; do not judge safety by tasting/drinking untreated water.
Replication and randomised/blind comparison reduce the chance a single fluke pour drives the decision.

title: Estimate longitude from local noon vs a GMT watch
scenario: Survivor needs their longitude and has a working digital watch still set to GMT/UTC (London winter time).
Clear sunny day; can push a straight stick vertically into the ground as a gnomon.
Needs the method to find local solar noon and the arithmetic to convert the time gap into degrees east/west.
prompt: My digital watch still works and is set to London time (GMT/UTC), but I'm lost and need my longitude. The sun is out and I can push a straight stick into the ground. How do I find 'local noon' here using the stick's shadow, and what's the exact arithmetic to turn the GMT time of local noon into how far east or west I am? Give me a worked example and tell me what limits the accuracy.
rubric: States the conversion: the Earth rotates 360 degrees in 24 hours = 15 degrees of longitude per hour (or 1 degree per 4 minutes).
Method: push the stick in vertically (plumb it), and mark the tip of its shadow every ~5-10 minutes through the middle of the day.
Identifies local solar noon as the moment the shadow is SHORTEST (sun highest), which also points true north-south.
Says to note the GMT time on the watch at that shortest-shadow moment.
States the direction rule correctly: if local noon occurs AFTER 12:00 GMT you are WEST; if BEFORE 12:00 GMT you are EAST of the Greenwich meridian.
Gives a correct worked example, e.g. local noon at 14:00 GMT = 2 hours late = 2 x 15 = 30 degrees WEST.

Corrects with the equation of time: true (mean) noon at Greenwich is not always 12:00; the sundial can be up to ~+16 min (early Nov) or ~-14 min (mid Feb) off clock time, and this must be applied or the longitude will be off by up to ~4 degrees.
Suggests improving the noon estimate by averaging the times of two equal-length shadows (one before, one after noon) rather than eyeballing the single shortest.
Quantifies sensitivity: a 4-minute timing error = 1 degree longitude (~111 km of east-west error at the equator), so timing must be careful.
Notes a longitude error budget / limitation: total accuracy depends on watch accuracy, the equation-of-time correction, and how precisely the shortest shadow is pinned.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Reverses the east/west rule (says local noon after GMT noon means EAST), which sends the position the wrong way.
Uses a conversion other than 15 degrees per hour (e.g. says 15 degrees per minute, or omits the time-to-angle conversion entirely).
reference_facts: Earth rotates 15 degrees of longitude per hour = 1 degree per 4 minutes of time.
Local solar noon = shortest shadow / sun's highest point; the noon shadow lies on the true north-south line.
Local noon later than 12:00 GMT => west of Greenwich; earlier => east.
Equation of time ranges roughly +16 min 33 s (~3 Nov) to -14 min 6 s (~11 Feb); it must be applied to clock-based noon.
A 4-minute timing error equals 1 degree of longitude (~111 km east-west at the equator).

title: Find latitude from solar-noon altitude with declination correction
scenario: Northern-hemisphere survivor needs to establish latitude to plan travel and to set up a sundial/clock; no GPS, no almanac for the exact date.
Available: a homemade quadrant/protractor with a plumb line, a vertical gnomon, and approximate knowledge of the calendar date.
Constraint: must correct for the sun's declination, which changes with the date, and must avoid the classic refraction and sign-of-declination errors.
prompt: I have no GPS and need to work out my latitude in the northern hemisphere using the sun at local noon. I have a homemade quadrant with a plumb line and a vertical gnomon. Walk me through it: how do I find the exact moment of local apparent noon (not clock noon), and how do I measure the sun's altitude safely without looking at it? Give me the full latitude formula relating my measured noon altitude and the sun's declination, with the correct sign convention for declination, and show me a worked example. Tell me how to get the sun's declination for today without an almanac — give me the value range it can take and a usable approximation from the calendar date, including the values at the equinoxes and both solstices. Also tell me the corrections I must apply for atmospheric refraction near the horizon and for the gnomon shadow/limb so I don't bias the result, and how accurate I can realistically expect to be in degrees.
rubric: States that local apparent noon is when the sun is due south (highest in the sky / shortest gnomon shadow), not 12:00 clock time, and is found from the shortest shadow / symmetric morning-afternoon equal-altitude method.

Gives the latitude formula for a southern-transit sun: latitude = 90° − (noon altitude) + declination, with declination signed (positive when sun is in the northern hemisphere, negative when in the southern).
Equivalently states zenith distance z = 90° − altitude and latitude = z + declination (so latitude = declination + (90° − altitude)).
States the sun's declination ranges from about +23.44° (June solstice) to −23.44° (December solstice) and is ~0° at the equinoxes (around 20–21 Mar and 22/23 Sep).
Gives a usable date approximation for declination, e.g. δ ≈ −23.44°·cos(360°/365·(N+10)) where N is the day of the year, or an equivalent table/curve.
Gives a correct worked example, e.g. equinox (δ=0) with noon altitude 38.5° → latitude ≈ 51.5° N, with arithmetic consistent with the stated formula.
Instructs measuring the sun's altitude indirectly/safely (project the gnomon shadow or use a pinhole/shadow on the quadrant) rather than looking directly at the sun, to protect the eyes.
States that atmospheric refraction raises the sun's apparent altitude (largest near the horizon, ~0.5° at the horizon), so noon altitude is best taken when the sun is high to minimise this error.

Notes that a measured altitude is to the sun's centre or a defined limb, and that small instrument errors (plumb-line/level, reading the scale) dominate, giving realistic accuracy of roughly ±0.25–1° (≈ tens of km).

Provides a verification/cross-check, e.g. repeat measurement on consecutive days and confirm latitude is consistent after applying the changing declination, or check against a known landmark/known latitude if available.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits the declination term entirely (e.g. claims latitude = 90° − noon altitude regardless of date), which is only true at an equinox.
Tells the user to measure the sun's altitude by looking directly at the sun through the sight without any eye protection or indirect/shadow method.
reference_facts: At local apparent noon the sun crosses the meridian (due south for a northern-hemisphere mid-latitude observer); shadow is shortest.
Zenith distance z = 90° − measured altitude; latitude = z + declination = (90° − altitude) + δ, with δ signed (+ toward the observer's hemisphere).
Solar declination ranges +23.44° at the June solstice to −23.44° at the December solstice, ≈0° at the equinoxes (~20–21 Mar, ~22–23 Sep).
Approximation: δ ≈ −23.44°·cos(360/365·(N+10)) with N = day of year (gives ~0° near day 80, +23.44° near day 172, −23.44° near day 355).
Atmospheric refraction raises apparent altitude by about 0.5° (≈34 arcmin) at the horizon, decreasing rapidly with altitude; measuring near transit (high sun) minimises it.
Worked check: equinox, noon altitude 38.5°, δ=0 → latitude = 90 − 38.5 + 0 = 51.5° N (consistent with London).

title: Fix position by map resection from three compass bearings
scenario: A scout with a topographic map and a baseplate/lensatic compass needs to pinpoint their unknown position to navigate back to a cache.
Available: map with three identifiable distant landmarks (a peak, a church spire, a lone tower), a protractor/baseplate compass, and a known local magnetic declination.
Constraint: compass reads magnetic north but the map grid is true/grid north; must convert correctly and avoid the resection geometry trap of near-collinear landmarks.
prompt: I'm lost but I can see three landmarks I can identify on my topographic map: a mountain peak, a church spire, and a lone tower. I have a baseplate compass and the map says local magnetic declination is 8° East. Tell me step by step how to fix my position by resection. Be precise about: how to take an accurate magnetic bearing to each landmark; how to convert magnetic bearings to map/true bearings given an 8° East declination (which way do I add or subtract?); how to convert each bearing to its back-bearing to draw a line FROM the landmark on the map; and how to plot the three lines. Explain what the small 'cocked hat' triangle where the three lines cross tells me and how I should treat my actual position relative to it. Why is taking three bearings better than two, and which landmark geometry should I AVOID choosing because it gives a weak, ambiguous fix? Also tell me the one thing that will wreck the whole fix if I ignore it.
rubric: Specifies taking each magnetic bearing by aiming the compass directly at the landmark, holding it level, and letting the needle settle, reading to the nearest ~1–2°.

Converts magnetic to true/map bearing correctly for EAST declination: add the easterly declination to the magnetic bearing (true = magnetic + 8°), and states the 'east is least/add east' (or equivalent) rule.
Computes each back-bearing by adding 180° if the bearing is <180° or subtracting 180° if ≥180° (i.e. reciprocal bearing), to draw the line outward from the landmark.
Instructs plotting each line of position from the landmark on the map along its back-bearing, using a protractor referenced to map/grid north.
States that the three lines generally form a small triangle ('cocked hat'); the true position lies within (or near) it and the fix is taken at its centre.
States that a large cocked hat indicates a measurement error (or wrong declination handling) and the bearings should be re-taken, not averaged blindly.
Explains that three bearings (vs two) provide a redundancy/error check — two lines always cross at a single point with no indication of error, whereas a third reveals it as the triangle size.

Identifies the geometry to avoid: landmarks that are nearly in line with each other / clustered in one direction, giving lines that intersect at shallow (near-parallel) angles and a weak, ambiguous fix; choose landmarks spread roughly 60–120° apart in azimuth.

Names the critical single error as failing to apply (or mis-applying the sign of) magnetic declination — an 8° error shifts the whole fix substantially — or holding the compass near iron/steel (local deviation).

Gives a verification/sanity check, e.g. confirm the plotted position is consistent with visible terrain/contours (on the right side of a ridge, near a stream), and re-shoot a bearing to confirm the triangle is small.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Subtracts an east declination (or otherwise inverts the declination sign) so the converted true bearings are wrong by 16° in the wrong direction.
Plots the line in the direction of the bearing (toward the landmark from an unknown point) instead of the back-bearing from the landmark, making resection impossible.
reference_facts: Resection fixes an unknown position from bearings to two or more identified known points; three gives an error-checking redundancy.
Magnetic-to-true conversion: with EAST declination, true bearing = magnetic bearing + declination (add east); with WEST declination, subtract.
Back-bearing (reciprocal) = bearing + 180° if bearing < 180°, else bearing − 180°; lines are plotted from each landmark along the back-bearing.
Two lines always intersect at one point with no error indication; three (or more) lines form a 'cocked hat' triangle whose size reflects measurement error, with the fix at its centre.
Strong fixes use landmarks separated by roughly 60–120° in azimuth; near-collinear/clustered landmarks give shallow intersection angles and a weak, ambiguous fix.
Failing to apply declination (here 8°) or compass deviation from nearby iron/steel biases every bearing and shifts the whole fix.

title: Generate a true flat reference surface with the three-plate method
scenario: **Situation:** A person needs a genuinely flat reference surface for precision layout, but owns nothing already known to be flat.
**Resources:** Three roughly flat cast-iron plates, hand scrapers, a marking medium (engineer's blue, soot, or thin oil+pigment).
**Goal:** Make at least one plate a true plane to use as a master reference.
prompt: I have three old cast-iron plates that are roughly flat, and I need one of them to be a truly flat reference surface for layout work. I have hand scrapers and some marking blue, but nothing already flat to check against. Walk me through how to make these flat using each other. Specifically: why do I need three plates and not just two? What's the exact order I compare and scrape them in? And how do I find and remove the high spots without making things worse?
rubric: Explains why two plates are insufficient: rubbed together they will happily mate as a matching convex/concave pair (a sphere/cylinder), which prints perfectly but is NOT flat.

States the principle: comparing three surfaces in rotation forces the only shape that mates with all the others to be a true plane — a flat surface is generated, not copied from an existing reference.
Describes the comparison cycle across all three pairings: A–B, A–C, and B–C (every plate is checked against every other), not just one fixed pair.
Describes using the marking medium (engineer's blue / soot / oil+pigment) thinly on one plate, rubbing the mating plate, and reading the transferred marks: marked patches are the HIGH spots.
Specifies scraping ONLY the high (marked) spots in small amounts and re-checking — you remove material from highs, you never try to add to or 'file down' the lows.
Specifies rotating/shifting the plates a random/varying amount between rubs (not the same orientation each time) to avoid generating a regular ridged/lobed 'false-flat' surface.
Describes iterating: as scraping proceeds the blue prints spread into more, smaller, evenly distributed spots covering the whole face — increasing points-per-area is the progress metric.
Notes the heat/handling hazard: hands, friction and sunlight warm cast iron and distort the reading; let plates settle to a common temperature and minimise handling before final comparisons.
Gives a concrete acceptance/verification check: the finished master shows uniform, closely spaced bearing spots across the whole surface when blued against either of the other two plates.

Gives an explicit watch/stop condition, e.g. 'stop and re-rub if marks concentrate only in bands or corners (a sign of curvature or a temperature gradient) rather than scraping blind', named not generic.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Says two plates rubbed together are sufficient to produce a true flat.
Tells the user to grind/sand/file the surface randomly or to remove material from the low (unmarked) areas instead of scraping the marked high spots.
reference_facts: Two surfaces lapped together mate as a complementary convex/concave pair and need not be flat; three surfaces compared in rotation can only be mutually flat when each is a true plane.
The Whitworth three-plate method compares all three pairings (A-B, A-C, B-C), using a marking medium where transferred marks indicate high spots to be scraped.
Relative orientation should be varied/randomised between comparisons to prevent generating a regular lobed or ridged surface that prints well but isn't flat.
Heat from handling, friction, and sunlight expands cast iron and corrupts flatness comparisons; thermal equilibrium matters for precision work.

title: Measure stream discharge by the float method for a mill
scenario: Survivors are siting a small undershot waterwheel and need to know the stream's discharge (volume flow) to estimate power and whether flow is adequate in late summer.
Available: a tape measure, a wooden float (a stick/orange), a marked staff/ruler for depth, and a watch with a seconds hand.
The reach is a roughly straight, uniform channel a few metres wide with a stony bed; no current meter or weir plate exists.
They want a defensible number, not a guess, and an idea of how wrong it could be.
prompt: I need to measure how much water my stream actually carries (its discharge in litres per second or cubic metres per second) so I can size an undershot waterwheel and know if late-summer flow is enough. All I have is a tape measure, a floating stick, a marked staff to read depth, and a watch with a seconds hand. The bed is stony and the channel is fairly straight and uniform, about 3 m wide. Give me the full float-velocity-area method: (1) how to pick and lay out the measuring reach and the float travel distance; (2) exactly how to time the float and how many runs to average; (3) how to convert the SURFACE velocity I measure into the MEAN velocity, including the correction coefficient and what value to use for a stony vs smooth bed and WHY surface is faster; (4) how to measure the cross-sectional area properly by dividing the width into segments and reading depths; (5) the final equation that combines everything into discharge Q with correct units; and (6) the main error sources and how I'd sanity-check the answer. Work a concrete example with my 3 m wide channel so I can see the arithmetic and the units.
rubric: States the core equation discharge Q = velocity x cross-sectional area (Q = v_mean x A), with consistent SI units (m/s x m^2 = m^3/s, and notes 1 m^3/s = 1000 L/s).
Specifies converting surface velocity to mean velocity with a coefficient k, Q = k x v_surface x A, and gives k ~= 0.85 typically, ~0.8 (or lower) for a rough/stony bed and ~0.9 for a smooth bed.
Explains surface velocity exceeds mean velocity because friction at the bed and banks slows the lower/edge flow, so the float (riding the surface) over-reads and must be reduced by k.
Instructs choosing a straight, uniform reach of known length (e.g. ~5-10 m) and timing the float over that fixed distance, computing v_surface = distance / time.
Specifies repeating the float run several times (e.g. >=3-5 runs) and averaging, and ideally releasing floats at several positions across the width (centre faster than edges).

Describes measuring cross-sectional area by dividing the width into several equal segments (e.g. ~0.3-0.5 m wide), measuring depth at each, and summing segment areas (mean-section / mid-section method) rather than assuming a rectangle.
Works a concrete numeric example: e.g. v_surface = 8 m / 10 s = 0.8 m/s; mean depth ~0.4 m over 3 m width gives A ~= 1.2 m^2; Q = 0.85 x 0.8 x 1.2 ~= 0.82 m^3/s (~820 L/s).
Names the main error sources: wind pushing the float, the float catching eddies/edges, non-uniform/curved reach, and an inaccurate k coefficient.

Gives a sanity check: compare results against a second independent method (a temporary V-notch weir, a bucket-fill time at a constriction, or a salt-dilution estimate) and re-measure if they disagree badly.
Notes discharge varies seasonally and the measurement should be repeated at low flow (late summer) since that, not peak flow, limits the waterwheel's usable power.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Explicitly uses surface velocity as the final mean velocity after being asked for the correction, causing a serious overestimate.
Gives a dimensionally wrong discharge equation or inconsistent units (e.g. multiplying by width only and ignoring depth, or reporting Q in m/s).
reference_facts: Discharge Q = mean velocity x cross-sectional area; units m^3/s = (m/s)(m^2); 1 m^3/s = 1000 L/s.
Surface velocity exceeds depth-averaged mean velocity; mean = k x surface with k typically ~0.85 (about 0.8 or lower for rough/stony beds, 0.85-0.9 for smooth sand/mud beds).
Velocity is measured by timing a float over a known straight reach length: v = distance / time; multiple runs are averaged.
Cross-sectional area is best found by the mid-section/mean-section method: divide width into segments, measure depth in each, sum segment areas.
Worked example: v_surface 0.8 m/s, A 1.2 m^2, k 0.85 gives Q ~= 0.82 m^3/s (~820 L/s).
A calibrated V-notch or rectangular weir gives a more accurate independent discharge estimate for cross-checking the float method.
Usable hydro power is limited by the lowest seasonal flow, so discharge should be measured at late-summer low flow.

title: Plan grain rations for 60 people with weekly audits
scenario: Settlement of 60 people has 300 kg of grain as the main calorie store and no resupply in sight.
They have a balance/known measures and a notebook; the leader must set a ration and run weekly stock audits.
Goal: a defensible ration calculation, an audit routine, uncertainty handling, and adjustment rules.
prompt: We have 300 kg of grain and 60 people to feed, with no resupply coming. I need a solid plan to make this last as long as possible. How do I calculate a sensible weekly ration per person, and how do I run an 'audit' each week to check how much is really left? What should I do if we're eating it faster than planned, or if my starting weight of 300 kg was actually a bit off? Walk me through the numbers and the sanity checks.
rubric: Computes the total resource per person: 300 kg / 60 people = 5 kg of grain per person total (states this explicitly).

Picks a target duration and derives the ration from it, OR picks a daily ration and derives the duration, showing the arithmetic both ways (e.g. 300 days at ~16.7 g/person/day is absurdly low; a 0.5 kg/person/day ration lasts only 300 kg / 30 kg/day = 10 days).
Anchors the ration in energy: grain is very roughly ~3,400-3,600 kcal per kg (dry cereal), so ~0.5 kg/person/day grain ~= ~1,700-1,800 kcal, which is near a survival floor and not full needs.

States the adult energy reference (~2,000 kcal/day, ~1,800 minimum survival floor) and concludes grain alone cannot meet full needs for long, so it must be stretched with other food and a realistic shorter horizon set.
Applies a sanity check that flags nonsense: a computed ration implying ~200 kcal/day (starvation) or ~20,000 kcal/day (impossible) per person means a units or arithmetic error and must be re-done.
Designs the weekly audit as a physical re-measure: weigh the remaining grain on the balance (or count calibrated containers), record it, and compare to predicted remaining stock.
Computes the real consumption rate from the audit: (last week's stock - this week's stock) = grain actually eaten; divide by people and days to get true g/person/day.

Sets an explicit adjustment rule: if measured stock is running ahead of plan (less left than predicted), cut the per-person ration so remaining stock / new rate still reaches the target date; recompute every week (closed-loop control).

Handles the uncertain starting weight: treat 300 kg as +/- an estimated error, plan to the pessimistic (low) end, and let the weekly re-weighing correct the estimate rather than trusting the initial figure.
Keeps a written ledger (date, weight in, weight out, balance) and reserves a buffer/safety stock so a measurement error or bad week doesn't cause an abrupt run-out.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Makes a gross units/arithmetic error (e.g. concludes 300 kg feeds 60 people for years, or sets a ration of tens of kg/person/day) and does not catch it with a sanity check.
Sets a fixed ration with no weekly re-measurement or adjustment loop, so over-consumption is only discovered when the grain runs out.
reference_facts: 300 kg / 60 people = 5 kg grain per person total.
Adult energy need ~2,000 kcal/day (survival floor ~1,800); dry cereal grain ~3,400-3,600 kcal/kg, so ~0.5 kg/day ~= ~1,700-1,800 kcal.
Sanity bounds: a per-person ration implying ~200 or ~20,000 kcal/day signals a units/arithmetic error.
Stock control is closed-loop: weigh remaining stock weekly, compute actual consumption = previous - current, and re-derive the ration to hit the target date.
An uncertain starting weight is handled by planning to the pessimistic bound and correcting via repeated weighing, plus holding a buffer reserve.

title: Synchronise clocks between two settlements 2 km apart
scenario: Two settlements ~2 km apart, in line of sight, want their clocks on the same time to coordinate trade meetups and shared tasks.
No radios; available: a daytime light/mirror flash or smoke, a lamp/fire at night, a loud horn, and homemade clocks (water clock/pendulum).
Goal: a synchronisation protocol with stated accuracy, accounting for signal delay, plus a missed-signal fallback for fog.
prompt: There's another group of survivors about 2 km away, in clear line of sight. We want to meet for trade and coordinate tasks, but our homemade clocks keep drifting apart. With no radios, how can we get both settlements onto the same time? We have mirrors and smoke for day, a lamp or fire for night, and a loud horn. What's the best signalling method, what accuracy can we expect, and what do we do if we miss the signal because of fog?
rubric: Defines synchronisation as agreeing on a single shared reference EVENT: both sides set their clocks to a pre-agreed time at the instant they observe one common signal (e.g. a mirror flash at an agreed nominal moment).

Chooses LIGHT (mirror heliograph by day, lamp/fire flash by night) as the primary sync signal because light crosses 2 km essentially instantly (~7 microseconds), so there is effectively zero light-travel delay to correct.

Explicitly addresses SOUND delay: sound travels ~343 m/s, so a horn heard at 2 km arrives ~5.8 seconds late (2000/343); if sound is used as the signal, the receiver must ADD that ~6 s, or use sound only as an attention-getter, not the time mark.
Anchors the agreed sync moment to a natural shared cue both can independently observe (e.g. local noon / shortest shadow, or sunset), so the schedule is grounded even if a signal is missed.
Specifies a clear, unambiguous signal protocol: a defined sequence (e.g. a steady count then a sharp 'mark' flash), agreed in advance, so both sides know exactly which instant is 'the time'.
Adds confirmation/handshake: the receiving settlement flashes back an acknowledgement, and a missing acknowledgement triggers a repeat, so a one-way miss doesn't silently desync them.
Schedules REGULAR re-synchronisation (e.g. daily at noon) because independent homemade clocks drift apart over hours/days; one sync is not enough.

States a realistic accuracy budget: human reaction time and reading a homemade clock limit sync to roughly +/-1-2 seconds (better than the ~6 s sound error), which is far finer than needed for coordinating meetups.

Handles missed signals / fog: fall back to the pre-agreed natural cue (next local noon/sunset) to re-sync, and build SLACK into meet-ups (a defined arrival window, not an exact instant) so a missed sync doesn't break the rendezvous.
Builds in redundancy: agree on a default plan if no contact for N days (e.g. 'meet at the midpoint at noon every 3rd day regardless'), so coordination survives a prolonged signalling outage.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Uses a sound signal over 2 km as the precise time mark with no correction for the ~6 s sound-travel delay.
Recommends a single one-time sync with no scheduled re-sync and no fallback for missed signals, ignoring clock drift.
reference_facts: Light travels ~3x10^8 m/s; over 2 km the delay is ~6.7 microseconds - negligible, so a light flash is an effectively instantaneous time mark.
Sound travels ~343 m/s in air (~20 C); over 2 km it arrives ~5.8 seconds late, which must be added if sound is the time signal.
Synchronisation requires a shared observable event plus a confirmation handshake; one-way signals can be silently missed.
Independent homemade clocks drift, so re-synchronisation must be periodic (e.g. daily) and anchored to a natural cue like local noon.
Practical sync accuracy with light + human reading is ~+/-1-2 s, ample for coordinating meet-ups, especially with a defined arrival window and a no-contact fallback plan.

title: Build a vernier scale to read 0.1 mm from a 1 mm ruler
scenario: Survivor is machining a part and needs to read dimensions to 0.1 mm.
Only tools available: a steel ruler marked in 1 mm divisions, a sharp scribe, and a flat strip of scrap metal to mark a sliding scale onto.
No calipers, no optics, no calculator.
prompt: I'm machining a part and my only ruler is marked in plain 1 mm divisions, but I need to read down to a tenth of a millimetre (0.1 mm). I have a sharp scribe and a flat strip of scrap metal I can scribe a sliding scale onto. How do I lay out and mark a sliding scale that lets me read 0.1 mm, exactly how many marks do I make and how far apart, and once it's built how do I actually take a reading and turn it into a number? Walk me through a worked example.
rubric: Identifies the device as a vernier scale (a sliding auxiliary scale read against the main 1 mm scale).
States the construction rule: take a span of exactly 9 mm on the strip (9 main-scale divisions) and divide it into 10 equal parts.
States that each vernier division is therefore 0.9 mm long, i.e. 0.1 mm shorter than each 1 mm main division.
Explains the reading principle: that 0.1 mm difference per division is what gives the 0.1 mm resolution (least count = main division minus vernier division = 1.0 - 0.9 mm).
Reading method: the tenths digit is read from WHICH vernier mark lines up flush with any main-scale mark (e.g. 3rd vernier line aligns -> 0.3 mm).
Reading method: the whole-millimetre part is read from where the vernier's zero (index) mark sits on the main scale, then the tenths from the aligned line are added.
Gives a correct worked example, e.g. vernier zero just past the 12 mm mark and the 4th vernier line aligns -> 12.4 mm.
Lays out the 10 divisions by stepping the 9 mm span and subdividing geometrically (or by stepping 0.9 mm), and scribes fine single lines, not wide grooves.
Notes that the vernier zero must be set as the true reference and the strip must slide parallel to / flush against the main scale to avoid parallax and skew error.
States the realistic limit: scribed-line and eye-alignment error means true accuracy is roughly +/-0.1 mm, not better, despite the 0.1 mm resolution.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Tells the survivor to just look closer at, magnify, or eyeball-interpolate the existing 1 mm marks instead of building a vernier.
Gives a construction that cannot resolve 0.1 mm (e.g. dividing 10 mm into 10 parts, or dividing 9 mm into 9 parts, so vernier and main divisions are equal).
reference_facts: Vernier principle: 9 main-scale divisions (9 mm) divided into 10 equal vernier divisions => each vernier division = 0.9 mm.
Least count = main-scale division - vernier-scale division = 1.0 mm - 0.9 mm = 0.1 mm.
The decimal (tenths) reading is given by which vernier line coincides with a main-scale line; the whole-mm part is read at the vernier zero/index.
Realistic field accuracy is limited by scribing/eye error to roughly +/-0.1 mm even though the resolution is 0.1 mm.

title: Set a clock to within minutes from a horizontal sundial
scenario: Survivor has a working horizontal sundial whose gnomon is angled to the local latitude and pointed at true (not magnetic) north; it reads apparent solar time.
They need to reset a hand-wound mechanical clock to civil/zone time to within a couple of minutes so shift handovers and a radio sked stay synchronised.
They know their longitude is 7 degrees WEST of the meridian that defines their time zone, the date is early November, and they are not on daylight saving.
No internet, no GPS, no radio time signal; only an almanac scrap giving the equation-of-time curve.
prompt: I have a horizontal sundial — the slanted gnomon is set to my latitude and aimed at TRUE north, and it reads the shadow time off a dial. I need to use it to reset my wind-up clock to proper zone (civil) time to within about 2 minutes, so our shift handovers and a daily radio call line up with the next settlement. Here is what I know: my location is 7 degrees of longitude WEST of the standard meridian that my time zone is based on; today is early November; and we are NOT on daylight saving. The sundial right now reads 11:50. Walk me through every correction I must apply, with the actual numbers and the correct SIGN of each, to turn that sundial reading into the clock time I should set. I specifically need: (1) why the sundial reading is apparent solar time and not clock time; (2) the longitude correction — how many minutes per degree and which way it goes for being west of my zone meridian; (3) the equation-of-time correction for early November, with its sign and roughly how big it is at its yearly extremes so I understand why it matters; (4) how to combine them into a final clock setting; and (5) how I'd check my result is right and what I should NOT do (e.g. magnetic vs true north, daylight saving). Give me the final clock time to set for a 11:50 sundial reading.
rubric: States the sundial shows APPARENT (true) solar time, and that to get clock/civil time you correct apparent solar time by the equation of time and by the longitude offset (plus any DST offset).
Gives the longitude correction as 4 minutes of time per degree of longitude (because 360 degrees / 24 h = 15 degrees per hour).
Applies the longitude correction correctly for being WEST of the zone meridian: the local sun lags the meridian, so you ADD ~28 minutes (7 deg x 4 min) to convert toward zone time.

States the early-November equation of time is near its yearly MAXIMUM at about +16 minutes (apparent ahead of mean), and applies it with the correct sign: SUBTRACT ~16 min when converting the sundial (apparent) reading to mean/civil time, because the sundial is running ~16 min fast.
Notes the equation of time swings roughly between about +16 min (early Nov) and about -14 min (mid-Feb) over the year, so it cannot be ignored for minute-level accuracy.

Computes a final clock setting consistent with its own stated signs: e.g. 11:50 + 28 min longitude - 16 min EoT ~= 12:02 (any answer must arithmetically match the candidate's own correctly-signed corrections).
Warns the gnomon/dial must be aligned to TRUE north, not magnetic north, and that magnetic declination would otherwise add a fixed time error.
States that if daylight saving were in force you would additionally ADD 60 minutes (and notes it is NOT in force here, so no DST term).

Gives a verification method: check at local apparent noon (shortest shadow / shadow pointing true north-south) that the corrected clock reads the expected civil time, or compare against a second independent time source.
Notes the sundial's accuracy limits: it is useless when overcast/at night, reads only to a few minutes, and the equation-of-time and longitude corrections must be re-checked as the date changes.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits the equation-of-time correction entirely (treats the sundial reading as already equal to mean/clock time).
Uses the wrong sign on either the longitude or equation-of-time correction so the final clock time is off by more than ~10 minutes.
Tells the user to align the sundial gnomon to magnetic north instead of true north.
reference_facts: Earth rotates 360 degrees in 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour = 4 minutes of time per degree of longitude.
West of the zone meridian the sun crosses later, so local apparent time lags zone time; you ADD 4 min per degree west to reach zone time (7 deg x 4 = 28 min).

With the equation of time defined as (apparent minus mean), local mean time = apparent solar time - equation of time; clock (zone/civil) time = apparent solar time - equation of time + longitude correction + DST offset.

The equation of time reaches about +16.5 minutes around 3 November (sundial ahead of clock) and about -14 minutes around 11 February; it also has minor extremes near +3.8 min (mid-May) and -6.5 min (late July).
A sundial must be oriented to true (geographic) north; using magnetic north introduces a fixed offset equal to the local magnetic declination converted to time.
Daylight saving time, when in effect, adds exactly 60 minutes to civil time relative to standard zone time.
Worked example: sundial 11:50 apparent + 28 min (longitude, west) - 16 min (Nov EoT, sun ahead) = about 12:02 civil time.

title: Compare: water-tube level vs A-frame level for establishing a true level line
scenario: Builders need to establish level reference points across a site — both matching two distant points to the same height and laying out contour lines on a slope.
They have clear tubing/hose and can build an A-frame with a plumb bob.
The asker wants to know which tool fits which job.
prompt: We need to get things level on our site — sometimes matching two far-apart points to the same height (for a foundation and a water channel), and sometimes marking level contour lines across a slope for terraces. We have clear hose and can build an A-frame with a plumb bob. Compare a water-tube level and an A-frame level — how each works, how accurate each is, and what each needs — and tell me which to use for which job.
rubric: Explains the water-tube level works by the hydrostatic principle: connected water settles to the same height at both open ends, so two points can be matched in level regardless of the terrain between them.
Notes the water level works around corners and over hills and dips and needs no line-of-sight, as long as the hose reaches between the two points.
States the critical requirement for the water level: no trapped air bubbles in the tube and both ends open to the air, or the readings are wrong.
States the water level is very accurate for matching two specific points to the same height (e.g. foundation corners, a tank, or a level irrigation channel).
Explains the A-frame level finds level (or marks a contour) between its two feet over only a short span — the width of the frame — using a plumb bob/string against a centre mark (or a bubble).
States the A-frame must be calibrated: mark where the plumb line crosses, then swap the legs end-for-end; the true centre is midway between the two marks.
Explains the A-frame is used by walking it along a slope, pivoting on one foot to find the next level point, to trace a continuous contour line (for terraces, swales, or ditches).
Identifies the A-frame's weakness: small errors accumulate step by step over a long run, and it is slow for matching two far-apart points.
Recommends the water-tube level for accurately matching two distant points to the same level (foundation, tank, long channel), especially around obstacles.
Recommends the A-frame for laying out continuous contour lines across a hillside (swales, terraces, contour ditches), where the goal is a level path rather than two fixed points.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the comparison.
Claims the water level requires line-of-sight or level ground between its ends, or that trapped air bubbles in the tube do not affect the reading.
reference_facts: A water-tube level uses hydrostatics: connected water settles to equal height at both open ends, matching two points in level regardless of intervening terrain and with no line-of-sight needed.
The water level requires no trapped air bubbles and both ends open to the atmosphere.
An A-frame level finds level/contour between its two feet over a short span using a plumb bob/string and a centre mark (or bubble); calibrate by swapping the legs end-for-end and taking the midpoint.
The A-frame is walked along a slope to trace continuous contour lines (terraces, swales); its errors accumulate over long runs.
Use the water level to match distant points; use the A-frame to lay out contour lines across a hillside.

title: Compare: shadow-stick (sun) vs Polaris (night) for finding true north
scenario: A traveller in the Northern Hemisphere has no working compass and needs to find true north, sometimes by day and sometimes at night.
They can use a vertical stick and its shadow by day, or the stars at night.
The asker wants the trade-offs in accuracy and when each works.
prompt: I have no compass and need to find north in the Northern Hemisphere — sometimes during the day, sometimes at night. Compare the shadow-stick method (using the sun) and using the North Star (Polaris) — how to do each, how accurate each is, when each works, and whether they give true or magnetic north — and tell me which to trust more.
rubric: Describes the shadow-stick method: set a stick upright, mark the tip of its shadow, wait (e.g. 15–30+ minutes), mark the new shadow tip; the line joining the marks runs roughly west-to-east.

States that in the shadow-stick method the FIRST mark lies toward the west and the second toward the east (because the shadow moves opposite to the sun), and the perpendicular to that line gives north-south.
Notes the shadow method works in daylight anywhere the sun shines, and is most accurate around solar noon / with a longer wait, but is only approximate.
Describes finding Polaris (the North Star) in the Northern Hemisphere using the two 'pointer' stars at the end of the Big Dipper's bowl (or using Cassiopeia on the opposite side).
States that facing Polaris means facing very nearly true north, because Polaris sits within about 1° of the celestial north pole.
Notes the Polaris method needs a clear night sky and works only in the Northern Hemisphere; in the Southern Hemisphere there is no bright pole star, so the Southern Cross is used instead.
Emphasises that BOTH methods give TRUE (geographic) north, not magnetic north, so they avoid the magnetic-declination error of a compass.
Notes Polaris is the more accurate of the two (it directly marks the pole) when visible, whereas the shadow-stick is a quicker but rougher daytime estimate.
Notes the altitude of Polaris above the horizon also approximates the observer's latitude (a useful cross-check).
Recommends Polaris for an accurate true-north fix on a clear night, and the shadow-stick for daytime or overcast nights, cross-checking the two when possible.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the comparison.
States that the first shadow-tip mark points east rather than west, reversing the derived north-south direction by 180°.
Claims the shadow-stick or Polaris method gives magnetic north or needs a declination correction (both give true north).
reference_facts: Shadow-stick: mark the shadow tip, wait, mark again; the line between marks runs roughly west–east, with the FIRST mark toward the west and the second toward the east; the perpendicular gives north–south. Most accurate near solar noon.
Polaris (Northern Hemisphere) sits within ~1° of the celestial north pole, so facing it is facing nearly true north; find it via the Big Dipper pointer stars or Cassiopeia.
The Southern Hemisphere has no bright pole star; the Southern Cross is used instead. Polaris needs a clear night sky.
Both methods give TRUE (geographic) north, not magnetic north, avoiding compass declination error.
Polaris's altitude above the horizon approximately equals the observer's latitude.

title: Investigate: a balance scale giving inconsistent weight readings
scenario: A balance scale gives different weights for the same object depending on who weighs it and when.
Accurate weights matter for rationing or formulating.
The asker wants to find the source of error and get reliable readings.
prompt: Our balance scale gives different weights for the same object depending on who weighs it and when. How do I find out where the error is coming from and get reliable, repeatable readings?
rubric: Advises checking the zero/tare first: with nothing on it (or just the empty container) the balance must read zero/balance; a non-zero or drifting start biases every reading.
Checks the balance is on a firm, LEVEL surface, since a tilted base throws off both balance- and spring-type scales.
Checks the reference masses/weights: worn, dirty, corroded, or wrong known weights make every comparison wrong — verify them against a trusted standard.
For a beam balance, checks the pivot/knife-edge and arms: friction, dirt, a bent or unequal arm, or a sticky pivot causes inconsistent or biased readings.
Identifies reading error: parallax (reading the pointer from an angle) and inconsistent eye position — advises reading square-on and at rest.
Identifies placement error: the object or weights not centred on the pan, or pans still swinging — let it settle and centre the load.
Distinguishes random scatter (different each time — friction, draughts, settling, reading) from a consistent bias (always high or low — zero offset, unequal arms, wrong reference weight).
Recommends a calibration check: weigh a known mass repeatedly and across the range, and use the substitution method (object, then known weights, on the same pan) to cancel unequal-arm error.
Controls the environment: draughts/wind, vibration, and a moving surface disturb a sensitive balance — shield and steady it.

Recommends fixing the identified cause (re-zero, level, clean/repair the pivot, verify/replace reference weights, read square-on, settle and centre loads) and re-testing repeatability before trusting it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the investigation.
Claims the scale needs no zeroing or calibration check and can be trusted without any comparison to a known mass.
reference_facts: Reliable weighing needs zeroing/taring (reads zero empty), a firm level surface, and verified reference masses; worn or wrong weights bias every reading.
Beam-balance errors: friction or dirt at the knife-edge/pivot, a bent or unequal arm, and loads not centred on the pan.
Reading errors: parallax (read square-on, at rest); environmental errors: draughts, vibration, an unsteady surface.
Random scatter (varies each time) points to friction/draughts/reading; consistent bias (always high/low) points to a zero offset, unequal arms, or a wrong reference weight.
Calibrate by weighing a known mass repeatedly across the range; the substitution method (object then known weights on the same pan) cancels unequal-arm error.

title: Investigate: checking whether a thermometer reads accurately
scenario: An operator suspects a thermometer is reading wrong but has no other trusted thermometer to compare against.
Accurate temperature matters for fermenting, cooking, or treating illness.
The asker wants to verify and correct it using known reference points.
prompt: I think one of our thermometers is reading wrong, but I have no other trusted thermometer to compare it with. How do I check whether it is accurate using things I can set up myself, and what do I do if it turns out to be off?
rubric: Recommends using fixed physical reference points that occur at known temperatures, rather than needing another trusted thermometer.
Gives the ice point: a well-stirred mix of crushed ice and water (an ice-water slurry) sits at 0 °C (32 °F) at normal pressure, so the thermometer should read 0 °C in it.
Gives the boiling point: pure water boils at 100 °C (212 °F) at sea level / standard pressure, so the thermometer in boiling water should read about 100 °C.
Notes the altitude correction: boiling point falls with altitude (roughly 1 °C per ~285 m, about 1 °F per ~500 ft), so the boiling reference must be adjusted for local elevation/pressure.
Describes the method: immerse the sensing bulb (not touching the container) in each reference, stir, let it stabilise, and record the reading against the known value.
Interprets the result: a consistent offset at both points indicates a fixed calibration error; different errors at the two points indicate a scale/non-linear problem.
Advises correcting by noting the offset and applying it to future readings, or re-marking/adjusting the scale if the instrument allows (some thermometers have a calibration nut).
Notes good technique: stir the bath, keep the bulb fully immersed but off the bottom and sides, and wait for the reading to settle.
Notes salt or impurities change the reference points (salt water freezes below 0 °C and boils above 100 °C), so use clean fresh water.
Recommends checking at least these two points (and ideally near the range you care about) and re-checking periodically, since thermometers drift.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the investigation.
States a grossly wrong reference temperature (e.g. that water freezes or boils at a value far from 0 °C / 100 °C at sea level), which would give a false calibration.
reference_facts: Calibrate against fixed points: an ice-water slurry = 0 °C (32 °F) at normal pressure; pure water boils at 100 °C (212 °F) at sea level/standard pressure.
Boiling point falls with altitude (~1 °C per ~285 m; ~1 °F per ~500 ft), so adjust the boiling reference for local elevation/pressure.
Method: immerse the bulb (not touching the container), stir, let it settle, and compare the reading with the known value at each point.
A consistent offset at both points = a fixed calibration error to apply; differing errors = a scale/non-linearity problem.
Use clean fresh water (salt/impurities shift the freezing and boiling points); thermometers drift, so re-check periodically.

title: Lesson plan: teaching accurate linear measurement and marking out
scenario: Workers need to measure and mark lengths accurately for building and making, where errors waste scarce material.
Rules/tapes and marking tools are available.
The asker wants a lesson on the technique and discipline of accurate measurement.
prompt: My workers keep getting measurements slightly wrong and wasting scarce material. I want to teach them to measure and mark lengths accurately and consistently with a ruler or tape. Design a lesson covering the technique, the common mistakes that cause errors, and how to check their accuracy.
rubric: States the objective: learners can measure and mark lengths accurately and consistently with a rule/tape.
Teaches using a consistent unit and a reliable, undamaged rule/tape, and understanding the scale's divisions.
Teaches measuring from the correct datum/zero: align the true zero with the start, and beware worn or damaged tape ends (or deliberately start from a clear mark, e.g. the '1', and subtract).
Teaches reading square-on to avoid parallax error (reading from an angle gives a wrong value).
Teaches keeping the tape/rule straight and taut along the true line, since a sagging or skewed tape over-reads the distance.
Teaches marking precisely with a fine, sharp line (a thick mark adds error) using a clear 'V'/crow's-foot at the exact point.
Teaches 'measure twice, cut once': re-checking a measurement before committing to an irreversible cut.
Teaches accounting for the width of the saw cut (kerf) and of the marking tool when laying out parts.
Teaches avoiding cumulative error: for several equal lengths, measure each from one datum (or mark them all at once) rather than measuring end-to-end repeatedly, which compounds small errors.
Uses hands-on practice with feedback and checks competence by having learners measure, mark, and cut to a tolerance.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting a usable lesson plan.
Teaches measuring without a correct zero/datum and without any re-check, presenting systematically inaccurate technique as correct.
reference_facts: Accurate measurement uses a consistent unit and a reliable, undamaged rule/tape read square-on (avoiding parallax).
Measure from the true zero/datum; beware worn tape ends — align the real zero or start from a clear mark (e.g. '1') and subtract.
Keep the tape/rule straight and taut along the line (a sagging/skewed tape over-reads); mark with a fine, sharp line at the exact point.
'Measure twice, cut once'; account for the saw kerf and marking-tool width when laying out parts.
Avoid cumulative error: measure several equal lengths each from one datum (or mark all at once) rather than end-to-end repeatedly.

title: Lesson plan: teaching calibrated use of body-based measures
scenario: People often have no ruler or tape in the field and must estimate distances and lengths with their body.
A measured baseline and a real ruler/tape are available for calibration practice.
The asker wants a lesson teaching how to use AND calibrate body-based measures.
prompt: Out in the field we often have no ruler or tape and have to estimate distances and lengths using our bodies — paces, hand-spans and so on. I want to teach people to do this well. Design a lesson on using body-based measures and, importantly, how to calibrate them so the estimates are actually useful, plus how to check their accuracy.
rubric: States the objective: learners can use and calibrate body-based/improvised measures for practical field estimation.
Teaches the common body measures: pace/step, foot length, hand-span, cubit (elbow to fingertip), thumb width, and arm-span — and that they are always available.
Teaches the key limitation: body measures vary from person to person (and with terrain and fatigue), so each person must calibrate their OWN against a known standard.
Teaches calibration: measure a known reference distance/length with a real ruler/tape, then count how many of your paces/spans fit it to find your personal conversion (e.g. metres per pace).
Teaches improving accuracy by averaging over many repetitions (e.g. pacing a long known distance several times and averaging) rather than relying on a single step.
Teaches counting technique: counting double-paces (every other step) for long distances to reduce miscounting, and keeping a natural, consistent stride.
Teaches recording the personal conversions and re-checking them periodically and on different ground (uphill/rough terrain shortens a pace).
Teaches that these measures are for ESTIMATION, not precision, and when a true measuring tool should be used instead.
Uses hands-on practice: learners calibrate their own pace/span against a measured baseline, then estimate an unknown distance and check the error.
Checks competence by having learners estimate a distance/length, compare it to the true value, and reflect on their accuracy.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting a usable lesson plan.
Claims body measures (pace, hand-span) are the same for everyone and need no personal calibration, so distances can be read off directly without error.
reference_facts: Common improvised measures (pace, foot, hand-span, cubit, thumb width, arm-span) are always available but vary from person to person, so each person must calibrate their own against a known standard.
Calibrate by measuring a known reference with a real ruler/tape and counting how many paces/spans fit it (e.g. metres per pace); average over many repetitions for accuracy.
For long distances count double-paces and keep a consistent natural stride; record conversions and re-check on different ground (uphill/rough terrain shortens a pace).
Body measures are for estimation, not precision; use a true measuring tool when accuracy matters.
Assess by estimating a distance/length and comparing it to the true value.

title: Troubleshoot: a pendulum clock running consistently fast or slow
scenario: A pendulum clock keeps consistently poor time — gaining or losing several minutes a day.
Knowing the time matters for coordination and schedules.
The asker wants to correct the rate and understand why.
prompt: Our pendulum clock keeps bad time — it gains (or loses) several minutes every day. How do I work out which way it is wrong and adjust it so it keeps good time? Explain what actually controls the rate.
rubric: States the key principle: a pendulum clock's rate is set by the pendulum's effective LENGTH — a longer pendulum swings slower, a shorter one faster (period ∝ √length).
Gives the correct fix direction: if the clock runs FAST, LENGTHEN the pendulum (lower the bob); if it runs SLOW, SHORTEN it (raise the bob).
Identifies the adjustment mechanism: the rating nut/screw under the bob (or moving the bob) changes the effective length in small steps.
Notes the period is (to a good approximation) independent of the bob's mass and, for small swings, of the amplitude — so adding weight does not fix timekeeping.
Recommends measuring the error over a known interval (e.g. seconds gained/lost per day) to know how much and which way to adjust, then adjusting a little and re-checking.
Identifies TEMPERATURE effects: the rod expands when warm (longer → slower) and contracts when cold (shorter → faster), causing seasonal drift.
Checks that the clock is LEVEL and solidly mounted, since being off-level or a wobbly mount disturbs the escapement and the rate.
Checks the escapement/movement: friction, dirt, lack of lubrication, or insufficient drive (weight/spring) can make it run irregularly or stop — distinct from a steady fast/slow rate.
Distinguishes a steady rate error (fix by pendulum length) from irregular or stopping behaviour (a mechanical/escapement/drive problem).
Recommends an iterative approach: measure the daily error, adjust the pendulum length the correct way by a small amount, re-measure, and repeat until accurate, accounting for temperature.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting to troubleshoot.
Gives the wrong fix direction (e.g. says to lengthen the pendulum to make a slow clock run faster), or claims adding mass to the bob corrects the timekeeping.
reference_facts: A pendulum's period depends on its effective length (T = 2π√(L/g)); longer = slower, shorter = faster; the period is independent of mass and (for small swings) amplitude.
Fix direction: clock runs FAST → lengthen the pendulum (lower the bob); runs SLOW → shorten it (raise the bob), via the rating nut/screw under the bob.
Measure the error over a known interval (seconds/day), adjust a small amount the correct way, and re-check iteratively.
Temperature drift: the rod expands when warm (slower) and contracts when cold (faster); keep the clock level and solidly mounted.
Distinguish a steady rate error (fix by length) from irregular/stopping behaviour (friction, dirt, lubrication, or insufficient drive in the escapement/movement).
